title: Sandford Fleming proposes worldwide standard time zones and a single universal day
body: Sandford Fleming (presented to the Canadian Institute, Toronto)
whatChanged: Fleming presented a scheme dividing the globe into 24 one-hour zones tied to the Greenwich meridian/anti-meridian, subordinate to a single 24-hour world clock he called 'Cosmic Time'. First influential, fully worked proposal for coordinated global zoned timekeeping and a common prime meridian.
technicalImpact: Conceptual ancestor of every modern offset-from-UTC model: a single global reference instant plus integer-hour regional offsets. Today's IANA tz zones and UTC offsets descend from this fan-out structure.

validation.supportingQuote: His radical plan created 'Cosmic Time,' a single tool to be used by the entire world... Soon, Fleming altered his plan to include a provision for dividing the world into 24 local time zones, designated by alphabet letters, which could be used alongside 'Cosmic Time.'

headline: A plan to give the world time zones
blurb: An engineer proposed splitting the globe into 24 one-hour zones tied to a single world clock.
story: In 1879 Sandford Fleming presented a plan to divide the world into 24 zones, each one hour apart. He tied them all to one shared 24-hour clock he called Cosmic Time. It was the first complete plan for coordinated global timekeeping. The way we set clocks by region today grew out of this idea.

title: US/Canada railways adopt Standard Railway Time ('Day of Two Noons')
body: General Time Convention of US/Canadian railroads (proposal by William F. Allen)
whatChanged: At noon on 18 November 1883, North American railroads switched from ~50+ local solar times to five standard zones based on mean solar time at the 60th, 75th, 90th, 105th and 120th meridians west of Greenwich, each one hour apart. Clocks in the eastern part of each zone were reset, producing two noons in one day.
technicalImpact: First large-scale operational use of discrete time zones with fixed hour offsets from a reference meridian, the data model every scheduling, transport, and timestamp system still uses. Established that civil time is an administrative convention, not astronomy, requiring a database of human-decided rules.

validation.supportingQuote: On November 18, 1883 individual railroad station clocks were reset as standard-time noon was reached within each time zone, hence the Day of Two Noons... The railroads instead implemented a version proposed by William F. Allen... whose system had five time zones named Intercolonial, Eastern, Central, Mountain, and Pacific.

headline: The day with two noons
blurb: North American railroads dropped dozens of local times for five shared zones, so some clocks hit noon twice.
story: Before 1883 towns kept their own local time, leaving railroads to juggle more than 50 versions of the clock. On November 18 the railroads switched everyone to five standard zones, each one hour apart. As the change took hold, clocks in parts of each zone were reset and noon happened a second time. It showed that the time on a clock is a human decision, not just the position of the sun.

title: International Meridian Conference adopts Greenwich as the prime meridian and defines the universal day
body: International Meridian Conference, Washington D.C. (convened by US President Chester A. Arthur)
whatChanged: 41 delegates from 25 nations adopted seven resolutions: a single prime meridian; Greenwich as the initial meridian (Resolution 2 carried 13 Oct, 22 ayes to 1); longitude counted east and west to 180 degrees; and a 'universal day' as a mean solar day beginning at mean midnight at Greenwich. Final resolutions adopted 22 October 1884.
technicalImpact: Fixed longitude zero at Greenwich and anchored the universal day to midnight Greenwich, the basis for GMT/UT and ultimately UTC as the zero-offset reference. Every UTC offset and IANA zone traces to these resolutions.

validation.supportingQuote: "The conference was held at the request of U.S. President Chester A. Arthur." / "Twenty-six countries, represented by 41 delegates, participated in the conference." / Resolution 2: "the adoption of the meridian passing through the centre of the transit instrument at the Observatory of Greenwich as the initial meridian for longitude. (Ayes, 22; noes, 1; abstaining, 2.)" / "That from this meridian longitude shall be counted in two directions up to 180 degrees..." / "That this universal day is to be a mean solar day; is to begin for all the world at the moment of mean midnight of the initial meridian." / "On 22 October 1884, the following resolutions were adopted by the conference (voting took place on 13 October)."
validation.corrections: whatChanged: change "41 delegates from 26 nations" to "41 delegates from 25 nations" (independent sources, Royal Observatory Greenwich and thegreenwichmeridian.org, state 25 nations).

headline: The world agrees where time starts
blurb: Delegates from many nations chose Greenwich as the starting line for measuring time and longitude.
story: In 1884, delegates from 25 nations met in Washington to pick one starting point for the world's clocks and maps. They chose the line through Greenwich, England. They also agreed that a universal day begins at midnight there. This choice still anchors how we measure time everywhere today.

title: Eucla and the Nullarbor roadhouses use the unofficial UTC+8:45 (Central Western Standard Time)
body: Local communities in Western Australia / South Australia (no statutory basis)
whatChanged: A small strip around Eucla on the Nullarbor keeps an offset of UTC+8:45 ('Central Western Standard Time', CWST), splitting the difference between Western Australia (UTC+8) and South Australia (UTC+9:30). Not legally defined by any government but recognized locally; IANA Australia/Eucla encodes a standard offset of 8:45.
technicalImpact: A 45-minute offset with no legal backing, a reminder that tz identifiers exist for de-facto practices. Its quarter-hour offset breaks whole/half-hour assumptions and is a frequently-overlooked edge case in zone-selection logic.

validation.supportingQuote: Zone Australia/Eucla 8:35:28 - LMT 1895 Dec / 8:45 Aus %z 1943 Jul / 8:45 AW %z, and the comments noting the "Central Western Time Zone is not officially recognised" across Nullarbor communities including "Cocklebiddy, Madura, Eucla and Border Village".
validation.corrections: Offset-change date: the UTC+8:45 standard offset for Australia/Eucla began 1895 December (per IANA zone line), not 1943-07-01. De-facto local use is documented from at least April 1935. The 1943-07 tzdb transition is only a DST rule-set change (Aus to AW) with no change to the 8:45 standard offset, so it should not be cataloged as an 'offset-change' adoption event. Suggested fix: date 1895-12 (or 'c. 1935' for documented local practice), and reclassify the 1943-07 entry as a DST-rules change rather than an offset change.

headline: A tiny town set its own quarter-hour time
blurb: A small community near the Nullarbor settled on a clock 45 minutes off from its neighbors.
story: The area around Eucla sits between two regions on different times. To split the difference, locals set their clocks to an unusual 45-minute offset. No government made it official, but the practice stuck and is still recorded in time software. Its odd quarter-hour setting often trips up programs that expect neat whole hours.

title: Standard Time Act of 1918 (Calder Act) - first US federal time law
body: US Congress (65th); enforcement delegated to the Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC)
whatChanged: First US federal law establishing standard time zones and a nationwide daylight saving time, adopting the railroad time-zone system into law. DST ran from the last Sunday in March to the last Sunday in October. The national DST mandate was repealed in 1919 over a presidential veto, leaving only the standard-time-zone framework.
technicalImpact: Established the legal definition of US time zones that every timekeeping system ultimately encodes. Root of the US standard-time offsets (EST -05, CST -06, MST -07, PST -08) the IANA tz database carries; America/* zones reference the 1918 Act.

validation.supportingQuote: The Standard Time Act of 1918, also known as the Calder Act, was the first United States federal law implementing Standard time and Daylight saving time. ... Signed into law by President Woodrow Wilson on March 19, 1918 ... authorized the Interstate Commerce Commission to define the limits of each time zone ... [DST] repealed ... over President Woodrow Wilson's veto [in 1919].

headline: America's first national time law
blurb: Congress put the railroad time zones into law and added a nationwide summer clock change.
story: In 1918 the United States passed its first federal law setting standard time zones across the country. It adopted the zone system the railroads had been using. The law also added a national daylight saving period for part of the year. The summer clock change was repealed the next year, but the time zones stayed.

title: IAU adopts 'Universal Time' to disambiguate Greenwich Mean Time
body: International Astronomical Union (IAU)
whatChanged: GMT was ambiguous because the astronomical day historically began at noon while civil GMT began at midnight. In 1925 astronomers shifted the astronomical day to a midnight start, and in 1928 the IAU introduced 'Universal Time (UT)' as the unambiguous term for mean solar time at Greenwich with the day starting at midnight.
technicalImpact: Removed a 12-hour ambiguity baked into 'GMT' that still surfaces in software: standards, RFCs, and tz documentation prefer UTC and treat 'GMT' as a legacy alias rather than a precise specification.

validation.supportingQuote: In 1928 the International Astronomical Union changed the designation of the standard time of the Greenwich meridian to Universal Time. ... In 1925 GMT was adopted by astronomers so that the astronomical day began at midnight, the same time as the civil day.

headline: A clearer name for Greenwich time
blurb: Astronomers introduced Universal Time to settle confusion about when the day begins.
story: Greenwich Mean Time was confusing because astronomers and everyday people once started the day at different hours. Astronomers had already moved their day to begin at midnight. In 1928 they introduced the name Universal Time to mean Greenwich time with a clear midnight start. Software today still favors this clearer naming over the older term.

title: Newfoundland keeps a UTC-3:30 standard offset (only half-hour zone in the Americas)
body: Dominion / Province of Newfoundland (Canada)
whatChanged: Newfoundland uses a standard offset of UTC-3:30 (Newfoundland Standard Time), based on the mean solar time of 52°30' W, a legacy of its status as a separate Dominion. IANA America/St_Johns shows the offset moving to a clean -3:30 in 1935 (earlier entries carry the LMT-derived -3:30:52). Summer time is UTC-2:30.
technicalImpact: Defeats any assumption that North American offsets are whole hours. The DST transition lands at a :30 boundary, so naive 'spring forward at 2am to 3am' logic misfires. A long-standing test case in datetime libraries.

validation.supportingQuote: Zone America/St_Johns	-3:30:52 -	LMT	1884 ... -3:30:52 StJohns N%sT	1935 Mar 30 / -3:30	StJohns	N%sT	1942 May 11

headline: Newfoundland keeps its half-hour clock
blurb: Newfoundland uses a time that sits on a half-hour, unlike the rest of the Americas.
story: Newfoundland was once a separate dominion and set its own clock to match its location. The result is a time offset that falls on the half-hour rather than a whole hour. In 1935 it settled on this tidy half-hour setting. Because its clock changes land on the half-hour, it often breaks software that assumes clean whole-hour jumps.

title: WWII year-round 'War Time' daylight saving
body: US federal government under President Franklin D. Roosevelt; enacted by Congress (Public Law 77-403)
whatChanged: Instituted year-round daylight saving time nationwide as a wartime energy-conservation measure, in effect continuously from 1942-02-09 to 1945-09-30. Zones were renamed Eastern/Central/Mountain/Pacific War Time, each one hour ahead of standard time.
technicalImpact: A multi-year stretch of permanent +1 offset that any historically accurate timezone library must encode as distinct transitions; IANA tz America/* zones carry these 1942-1945 'War Time' transitions. Getting them wrong corrupts conversions of WWII-era timestamps.

validation.supportingQuote: "On February 9, 1942, 'War Time', a year-round daylight saving time, began in the United States. Passed by Congress and signed into law by President Franklin D. Roosevelt ... as a national defense measure to conserve energy ... in force continuously from February 9, 1942 to September 30, 1945."

headline: America kept summer time all year in wartime
blurb: During World War Two the United States stayed on daylight saving time year-round to save energy.
story: From 1942 to 1945 the United States kept clocks on daylight saving time all year long. It was meant to save energy during World War Two. The zones were even renamed to War Time during this stretch. Software that handles old dates from these years has to account for this long, unbroken change.

title: Chatham Islands use UTC+12:45 (Chatham Standard Time), 45 minutes ahead of NZ
body: Government of New Zealand
whatChanged: The Chatham Islands settled on a standard offset of UTC+12:45, 45 minutes ahead of New Zealand. IANA Pacific/Chatham encodes 12:45 from 1946-01-01 with a DST rule that keeps it 45 minutes ahead of Auckland year-round (summer UTC+13:45). DST transitions occur at the odd local times 2:45 / 3:45.
technicalImpact: One of two surviving 45-minute offsets, and it also has DST, stacking a quarter-hour base offset with a one-hour summer shift (UTC+13:45). Transition instants land at :45 past the hour, breaking schedulers that assume whole-hour boundaries.

validation.supportingQuote: Zone Pacific/Chatham ... 12:45 Chatham %z (effective 1946 Jan 1); "Chatham is always exactly 45 minutes ahead of Auckland."

headline: Chatham Islands run 45 minutes ahead
blurb: These islands keep a clock 45 minutes ahead of New Zealand, and they still change for summer.
story: In 1946 the Chatham Islands settled on a clock 45 minutes ahead of mainland New Zealand. They also shift their clocks for summer like the mainland does. That makes their seasonal changes happen at unusual times, on the 45-minute mark. The combination of a quarter-hour gap and summer time often confuses scheduling software.

title: Pre-1972 UTC regime: frequency offsets and fractional-second 'rubber second' steps
body: Bureau International de l'Heure (BIH), under CCIR recommendations
whatChanged: From 1 January 1961 until end of 1971, UTC was kept close to UT2 not by integer leap seconds but by deliberately offsetting the broadcast frequency from the atomic second each year and inserting occasional step adjustments of fractional seconds (commonly 100 ms, with one 50 ms step). UTC seconds were not exactly equal to SI/TAI seconds.
technicalImpact: Precise UTC-TAI history before 1972-01-01 is non-integer and messy; libraries and timescale tables (SOFA/erfa, skyfield) carry special-case piecewise formulas with rate offsets for 1961-1972. Code assuming UTC seconds always equaled SI seconds is wrong for this decade.

validation.supportingQuote: From the IOPscience article landing page for DOI 10.1088/1681-7575/ab1e68, "The Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)," G Panfilo and F Arias, Metrologia 56(4), 2019, Section 2 "The history of UTC" addresses the pre-1972 regime: BIH (predecessor to BIPM, which assumed responsibility in 1988) maintaining UTC via frequency offsets and fractional-second steps before integer leap seconds were introduced in 1972, with the TAI offset and atomic-second relationship discussed in the UTC definition section.

headline: When seconds could stretch
blurb: Before 1972 world time was kept in sync by tiny, fractional adjustments instead of whole seconds.
story: From 1961 to 1971, world time was kept close to the Earth's rotation in an unusual way. Instead of adding a whole second, clocks were nudged by small fractions of a second and ran at a slightly tweaked rate. This meant a second back then was not exactly the same length as a second today. Software that works with this decade needs special handling for these uneven adjustments.

title: Uniform Time Act of 1966 standardizes US DST dates
body: US Congress (89th); administered by the US Department of Transportation
whatChanged: Ended two decades of state/local DST chaos by mandating uniform DST dates within each time zone: spring forward on the last Sunday in April, fall back on the last Sunday in October, effective 1967. States could exempt themselves entirely but could not set their own DST dates.
technicalImpact: Created the single nationwide DST rule set that became the canonical 'US' rule in the tz database. The legislative anchor that later acts (1986, 2005) amend; tz 'US' Rule lines encode these last-Sunday transitions for 1967 onward.

validation.supportingQuote: "The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) oversees the Nation's time zones... DOT regulations at 49 CFR part 71 contain the official listing of the Nation's time zones. Under the Uniform Time Act, as amended, States may exempt themselves from observing Daylight Saving Time... If a State chooses to observe Daylight Saving Time, it must begin and end on federally mandated dates."

headline: America fixes its summer clock dates
blurb: A 1966 law set one shared schedule for when clocks spring forward and fall back.
story: For years, states and towns started and ended daylight saving time on their own dates, causing confusion. In 1966 a federal law set uniform dates within each time zone. States could opt out entirely, but they could no longer pick their own dates. This shared schedule became the standard rule that later laws would adjust.

title: CCIR formally names the timescale 'Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)'
body: 13th Plenary Assembly of the CCIR (now ITU-R), Recommendation 460-1
whatChanged: The post-1972 atomic-based, leap-second-adjusted timescale was formally given the name 'Coordinated Universal Time' with abbreviation UTC in CCIR Recommendation 460-1 at the 1974 plenary assembly.
technicalImpact: Established 'UTC' as the single canonical name used by virtually every standard, protocol, and API (ISO 8601, RFC 3339, NTP, language time libraries). The reference timescale from which all IANA tz offsets are measured.

validation.supportingQuote: "RECOMMENDATION ITU-R TF.460-6 - Standard-frequency and time-signal emissions", Annex 1 defines "Coordinated universal time (UTC)" alongside UT and TAI, with Annex 2 on "Leap-seconds," issued by the ITU Radiocommunication Bureau (formerly CCIR).
validation.corrections: The name "Coordinated Universal Time" with abbreviation UTC was adopted in 1967, by CCIR and approved by IAU Commissions 4 and 31 at the IAU 13th General Assembly (1967), not in 1974. The leap-second-adjusted atomic UTC system was established by CCIR Recommendation 460 (XIIth Plenary Assembly, New Delhi, January 1970) and took effect 1 January 1972. CCIR Recommendation 460-1, issued at the 1974 plenary, revised the UT1-UTC tolerance from 0.7 s to 0.9 s; it did not introduce the UTC name. Suggested corrected event: title -> "UTC name and abbreviation adopted (CCIR/IAU)"; date -> 1967; whatChanged -> the timescale was formally named 'Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)' in 1967 (UTC chosen as a language-neutral compromise between English CUT and French TUC).

headline: The world's clock gets its name
blurb: International officials gave the global timescale its lasting name, Coordinated Universal Time.
story: The shared global timescale needed one agreed name. Officials settled on Coordinated Universal Time, shortened to UTC. The short form was a compromise that did not favor one language. That name is now used by nearly every standard and computer system that keeps time.

title: International Atomic Time (TAI) formally established by the CGPM
body: 14th General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM) / BIH, later BIPM
whatChanged: The CGPM formally recognized and named International Atomic Time (TAI), a continuous atomic timescale built from cooperating cesium clocks, with origin set so TAI approximately agreed with UT1 at 1 January 1958 00:00:00. TAI ticks the SI second uniformly with no leap-second adjustments.
technicalImpact: TAI is the monotonic, gap-free timescale that leap-second-aware systems use internally (tz 'right'/zoneinfo-leaps counts every TAI second). The fixed TAI-UTC integer offset is the quantity that changes on every leap second; GNSS and distributed databases effectively track TAI rather than UTC.

validation.supportingQuote: Ultimate consecration came from the official recognition by the 14th CGPM in 1971, which introduced the designation 'International Atomic Time' and the universal acronym TAI.

headline: A steady atomic clock for the world
blurb: Officials formally created a continuous timescale built from atomic clocks that never skips.
story: In 1971 the world formally recognized International Atomic Time. It is built from many atomic clocks working together and ticks at a perfectly steady rate. Unlike everyday time, it never pauses or adds a second to stay in step with the Earth. Many precise systems rely on this smooth, gap-free timescale behind the scenes.

title: Ireland models winter (GMT) as negative DST atop a UTC+1 'standard time'
body: Oireachtas (Standard Time Act 1968 / Standard Time (Amendment) Act 1971), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Irish law defines standard time as UTC+1 year-round; the 1971 amendment keeps UTC+1 as the legal standard observed in summer and designates GMT as 'winter time'. IANA Europe/Dublin is modeled with a standard offset of +1:00 (abbrev IST) and a negative SAVE of -1:00 in winter (abbrev GMT), the opposite sign convention from every other DST-observing zone.
technicalImpact: The only mainstream zone with negative DST. Code assuming the DST flag means 'clocks are ahead' or that SAVE >= 0 reports Ireland's seasons backwards. It broke ICU and OpenJDK test suites, forcing the tz project to ship parallel 'vanguard' and 'rearguard' data formats.

validation.supportingQuote: "Ireland's Standard Time (Amendment) Act, 1971 states that standard time in Ireland remains at UT +01." ... "In January 2018 we discovered that the negative SAVE values in the Eire rules cause problems with tests for ICU" ... IST = Irish Standard Time after 1968, with standard time in summer and negative daylight saving time in winter.

headline: Ireland flips daylight saving backward
blurb: Ireland treats winter as the time it pulls clocks back from, the reverse of how others do it.
story: Most places treat summer as the season they push clocks forward. Irish law instead defines its main time as the summer setting and treats winter as the time pulled back from it. The result is the same clock readings as its neighbors, but described in reverse. This backwards way of recording it has caused real problems in time software.

title: Modern UTC begins: TAI-UTC set to exactly 10s and the 1-second leap-second regime
body: CCIR (now ITU-R) Recommendation 460; implemented by BIH
whatChanged: At 1 January 1972 00:00:00, UTC was set to exactly TAI minus 10 seconds, abandoning the rubber-second/frequency-offset scheme. From then the UTC second equals the SI/TAI second exactly, and sync with UT1 is maintained only by whole-second leap steps keeping |UT1-UTC| within 0.9s, inserted preferentially 30 June or 31 December as the special second 23:59:60.
technicalImpact: Created the discontinuity that breaks naive software: a UTC minute can contain 61 seconds, and POSIX time_t cannot represent 23:59:60. Root cause of leap-second outages and the POSIX '86400 seconds/day' fiction. The IANA tz 'posix' config excludes leap seconds while 'right' counts them; the two diverge at the first leap second.

validation.supportingQuote: "A positive or negative leap-second should be the last second of a UTC month, but first preference should be given to the end of December and June... A positive leap-second begins at 23h 59m 60s and ends at 0h 0m 0s of the first day of the following month." (§2.1-2.2). "The departure of UTC from UT1 should not exceed ±0.9 s" (§1.2). "[UTC] corresponds exactly in rate with TAI but differs from it by an integer number of seconds. The UTC scale is adjusted by the insertion or deletion of seconds (positive or negative leap-seconds) to ensure approximate agreement with UT1" (Annex 1, C).

headline: Modern UTC and the leap second begin
blurb: In 1972 the world clock got a new rulebook, with occasional one-second steps to stay in sync.
story: On January 1, 1972, the world adopted a new way of keeping official time. Each second now matches a steady atomic count. To stay lined up with the spinning Earth, clocks add a single extra second once in a while. That extra second confuses software that assumes every minute has exactly sixty seconds.

title: Leap second #1 (first ever) inserted at 1972-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: Bureau International de l'Heure (BIH); now announced by IERS Bulletin C
whatChanged: The first-ever leap second was inserted following 1972-06-30 23:59:59 UTC, creating second 23:59:60 and moving TAI-UTC from 10 to 11 seconds. The first real-world application of the 1972 regime and the first deliberate 61-second minute.
technicalImpact: The exact instant where IANA tz 'right' (leap-aware) and default 'posix' time_t first diverge: time_t 78,796,800 maps to 1972-06-30 23:59:60 UTC under 'right' but is the following second under POSIX. The canonical test case for any leap-second-aware time library.
headline: The first leap second is added
blurb: In mid 1972 the world clock gained its first ever extra second.
story: At the end of June 30, 1972, timekeepers added one extra second to the official clock. It was the first leap second ever used. The minute ran from second 59 to a rare second 60 before rolling over. Many programs cannot handle that unusual sixty-first second.

title: Leap second #2 inserted at 1972-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 11s -> 12s. The only calendar year with two leap seconds.
technicalImpact: Same 86400-second-day assumption breakage as all positive leap seconds; second of two insertions in 1972, the only year with two.
headline: A second leap second in one year
blurb: Late 1972 added another extra second, the second one that year.
story: At the end of December 31, 1972, the world clock gained one more extra second. This was the second leap second of the year. So far 1972 is the only year that needed two. Each added second can trip up software that expects a fixed-length minute.

title: Emergency Daylight Saving Time Energy Conservation Act (1973 oil-crisis year-round DST)
body: US Congress (93rd); signed by President Richard Nixon (Public Law 93-182), administered by US DOT
whatChanged: In response to the 1973 oil crisis, Congress mandated year-round DST. Signed 1973-12-15; year-round DST began 1974-01-06. After backlash over dark winter mornings, Congress amended the Act 1974-10-05 and standard time resumed 1974-10-27.
technicalImpact: Produced irregular one-off DST transitions in 1974 (DST started January 6) and 1975 that diverge from the regular 1967-1986 rule. IANA tz encodes these as explicit single-year exceptions; a library naively applying the steady last-Sunday-April rule misconverts 1974/1975 US timestamps.

validation.supportingQuote: Signed into law by President Richard Nixon on December 15, 1973 ... Pub. L. 93-182 ... This trial period began on January 6, 1974, and was intended to end on April 7, 1975. Lawmakers ended the experiment early on October 27, 1974.

headline: Year-round daylight time during the oil crisis
blurb: To save energy in 1974, the US kept clocks on daylight time all winter.
story: After the 1973 oil crisis, the US decided to keep daylight saving time on all year. The change began in January 1974 and meant darker winter mornings. People pushed back, and the country returned to normal time later that year. The unusual one-off dates make 1974 and 1975 tricky for software that expects the regular schedule.

title: Leap second #3 inserted at 1973-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 12s -> 13s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 2335219200	13	# 1 Jan 1974 (offset becomes 13s, immediately following 2303683200	12	# 1 Jan 1973), confirming a leap second inserted at end of 1973 changing TAI-UTC 12s -> 13s.

headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1973 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1973, timekeepers added one extra second to the official clock. It was the third leap second so far. The step kept the clock in line with the slowly changing Earth. These added seconds need careful handling in time software.

title: Leap second #4 inserted at 1974-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 13s -> 14s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1974 added one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1974, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the fourth such adjustment. The step kept official time matched to the turning Earth. Software that ignores these seconds can drift out of step.

title: Leap second #5 inserted at 1975-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 14s -> 15s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1975 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1975, timekeepers added one extra second to official time. It was the fifth leap second. The small step kept clocks aligned with the Earth's rotation. These rare seconds remain a common source of bugs.

title: Leap second #6 inserted at 1976-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 15s -> 16s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1976 added one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1976, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the sixth such step. The adjustment kept official time in sync with the spinning Earth. Time software has to account for each added second.

title: Leap second #7 inserted at 1977-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 16s -> 17s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1977 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1977, timekeepers added one extra second to the official clock. It was the seventh leap second. The step kept clocks lined up with the Earth's slow changes. Each extra second can confuse programs that expect steady minutes.

title: Leap second #8 inserted at 1978-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 17s -> 18s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.

validation.supportingQuote: 2492985600 18 # 1 Jan 1979 (preceded by: 2461449600 17 # 1 Jan 1978), confirming TAI-UTC went 17s -> 18s at the 1978-12-31/1979-01-01 boundary, i.e. the leap second 1978-12-31 23:59:60 UTC.

headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1978 added one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1978, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the eighth such adjustment. The small step kept official time matched to the turning Earth. These seconds still need careful handling in software.

title: Leap second #9 inserted at 1979-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH / CCIR; IERS in later years
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 18s -> 19s.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; advances DTAI in NTP/tz leap tables.
headline: Another leap second is added
blurb: The end of 1979 brought one more extra second to the clock.
story: At the end of December 31, 1979, timekeepers added one extra second to official time. It was the ninth leap second. The step kept clocks aligned with the Earth's rotation. Software that tracks precise time must include each added second.

title: Leap second #10 inserted at 1981-06-30 23:59:60 UTC
body: BIH (Bureau International de l'Heure)

headline: A leap second arrives in June
blurb: Mid 1981 added an extra second, the first June step in nine years.
story: At the end of June 30, 1981, the world clock gained one extra second. It was the tenth leap second, and the first one set in June since 1972. The nine before it had all landed at the end of December. Handling a mid-year step tests parts of software that rarely run.

blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of June 30, 1985.

title: Lord Howe Island adopts a 30-minute DST shift (UTC+10:30 <-> UTC+11:00)
body: Government of New South Wales (Governor's order under the Standard Time Act 1971)
whatChanged: From summer 1985, Lord Howe Island's DST advances the clock by only 30 minutes (LH SAVE = 0:30), going from UTC+10:30 to UTC+11:00 rather than UTC+11:30. Before 1985 it used a full one-hour shift. IANA Australia/Lord_Howe encodes this with a dedicated 'LH' rule whose SAVE is 0:30.
technicalImpact: The canonical real-world case proving DST offsets are not always whole hours. Any library assuming DST always adds exactly 3600 seconds (or stores DST as a boolean) computes the wrong wall-clock time here.

headline: An island shifts clocks by half an hour
blurb: Lord Howe Island started moving its clocks just thirty minutes for summer instead of a full hour.
story: Most places that change clocks for summer move them by a full hour. Starting in 1985, Lord Howe Island chose to move its clocks by only thirty minutes. Software that always assumes a full hour gets the time wrong on this island.

title: Nepal shifts from UTC+5:30 to UTC+5:45 (Nepal Standard Time)
body: Government of Nepal
whatChanged: Nepal advanced its clocks 15 minutes from Indian Standard Time (UTC+5:30, used since 1920) to UTC+5:45, establishing an independent Nepal Standard Time effective 1986-01-01, nominally aligned to the meridian of Gauri Shankar. IANA Asia/Kathmandu encodes 5:30 until 1986, then 5:45.
technicalImpact: One of several quarter-hour (45-minute) offsets in use, alongside Chatham (UTC+12:45) and Eucla (UTC+8:45). Quarter-hour offsets break code storing offsets in whole/half hours, and any system deriving zone offset from longitude/15 mis-places Nepal. The 15-minute India difference causes cross-border scheduling bugs.

validation.supportingQuote: Nepal Standard Time has existed since January 1, 1986, when Nepal adopted the new time zone after they advanced their clocks by 15 minutes ahead of their previous time zone Indian Standard Time.

All other fields (date 1986-01-01, body, offset change, IANA encoding) are correct as written.

headline: Nepal set its own clock
blurb: Nepal moved its clocks fifteen minutes ahead to create its own national time.
story: On January 1, 1986, Nepal pushed its clocks fifteen minutes ahead of the time it had shared with India. This gave Nepal its own national time, tied to a peak in the country. The unusual fifteen minute step trips up programs that expect time differences in whole or half hours.

title: Creation of the Olson/zoneinfo (tz) database by Arthur David Olson
body: Arthur David Olson (US National Institutes of Health) and volunteer contributors
whatChanged: Olson created a structured, version-controlled compilation of the world's civil time-zone and daylight-saving rules in human-editable source form, compiled to binary zoneinfo. Earliest documented public reference is Olson's 1986-11-24 post. Zones are named Area/Location (e.g. America/New_York) and all agree about timestamps after the 1970 POSIX epoch.
technicalImpact: Became the de-facto time-zone reference for Unix, Linux, macOS, the JVM, glibc, Python, Ruby, ICU/CLDR, Oracle and countless applications. Any program resolving local time from UTC depends on it; an out-of-date copy silently produces wrong wall-clock times. Released to the public domain.

validation.supportingQuote: "occasionally as the Olson database, referring to the founding contributor, Arthur David Olson." ... "Olson, Arthur David (24 November 1986). 'seismo!elsie!tz ; new versions of time zone stuff'" ... "The database, as well as some reference source code, is in the public domain."

headline: The world's time zone database is born
blurb: An engineer started a shared, editable record of every country's clock rules.
story: In 1986 Arthur David Olson began collecting the world's clock and daylight saving rules into one organized, editable record. Each place got a clear name, like a region paired with a city. This collection became the trusted reference that phones, computers, and countless programs use to turn a moment into the right local time.

headline: A leap second was added in 1987
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of December 31, 1987.
story: An extra second was added to the official clock on December 31, 1987. This small adjustment kept clock time matched to the Earth's rotation. It was the last such second handled by the older timing office before a new group took over coordination.

title: IERS Bulletin C: the leap-second announcement mechanism
body: IERS (International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service), Earth Orientation Centre, Paris Observatory
whatChanged: Twice a year IERS publishes Bulletin C, which either announces a 1-second step in UTC at the next opportunity (after 23:59:59 UTC on June 30 or December 31) or confirms no step. Leap seconds may only be inserted at end of June or December.
technicalImpact: Bulletin C is the single authoritative trigger downstream consumers (NTP servers, IANA tzdb leap-seconds.list, OS vendors, GNSS operators) watch to update timekeeping. Its ~6-month lead time is why leap-second handling can be staged in advance; leap-seconds.list carries an expiration date tied to the latest Bulletin C.

validation.supportingQuote: "Contains announcements of the leap seconds in UTC" (IERS Bulletin C page); corroborating: "Bulletin C is mailed every six months, either to announce a time step in UTC, or to confirm that there will be no time step at the next possible date. The Earth Orientation Center is located at the Observatoire de Paris."

headline: A regular notice for leap seconds
blurb: A timing body began publishing a twice a year notice that says whether a leap second is coming.
story: Starting in 1988, a timing organization began issuing a notice twice a year called Bulletin C. Each notice either announces that one extra second will be added at the end of June or December, or confirms that none is needed. Everyone who keeps clocks accurate, from servers to satellite systems, watches this notice to know when to adjust.

title: Kiribati moves the Line Islands to UTC+14, creating the world's farthest-forward time zone
body: Government of the Republic of Kiribati
whatChanged: To put the whole country on the same calendar date, Kiribati moved its eastern Line Islands (including inhabited Kiritimati) from UTC-10:00 to UTC+14:00 at the end of 1994-12-31, bending the International Date Line eastward and making Pacific/Kiritimati the highest positive UTC offset in existence.
technicalImpact: UTC+14 breaks the common (wrong) assumption that valid offsets range only -12 to +12. Off-by-one errors appear in 'which day is it' logic, ISO-week calculations, and any UI clamping offsets to ±12h. A single UTC instant can span three different calendar dates worldwide.

validation.supportingQuote: Zone Pacific/Kiritimati ... -10:00 - %z 1994 Dec 31 / 14:00 - %z, with the comment that Kiribati "declared it the same day [throughout] the country as of Jan. 1, 1995".

headline: Kiribati jumps to the world's earliest time
blurb: Kiribati moved its eastern islands so the whole country shared one calendar date.
story: At the end of 1994, Kiribati moved its eastern islands forward so the entire country would be on the same calendar date. This gave those islands the earliest local time on Earth. It also broke a common but wrong assumption in software that time can never be more than twelve hours ahead of the global reference.

headline: Last extra second of the 1900s
blurb: Clocks gained one extra second at the end of 1998, and then none came for seven years.
story: On the last day of 1998, timekeepers added one extra second to the world clock. It was the final added second of the twentieth century. After it, no extra second was added for about seven years. That long quiet gap meant some computer systems never ran the code that handles these extra seconds.

title: GPS Week Number Rollover (10-bit WN), 1999 and 2019
body: U.S. Space Force / National Coordination Office for Space-Based PNT (GPS.gov)
whatChanged: The legacy GPS C/A-code navigation message broadcasts the week number in a 10-bit field (max 1,023). Counting from the GPS epoch (1980-01-06), it overflows every 1,024 weeks (~19.7 years), resetting to zero. First rollover 1999-08-21; most recent 2019-04-06 at 23:59:42 UTC.
technicalImpact: Receivers without a pivot/base-week offset compute a date ~19.7 years in the past, corrupting timestamps, almanac/ephemeris validity, and any system deriving wall-clock time from GPS. Modernized CNAV uses a 13-bit week number; legacy C/A devices face the next rollover 2038-11-20.

validation.supportingQuote: The main civil GPS signal (C/A code) broadcasts the GPS week number using a 10-bit code with a maximum value of 1,023 weeks. ... The last GPS Week Number Rollover occurred at 23:59:42 UTC on April 6, 2019.

headline: GPS week counter runs out and resets
blurb: The week counter used by GPS only goes so high, so it resets and can confuse some devices about the date.
story: GPS keeps track of time by counting weeks, but the count uses a small field that can only reach a certain limit. When it fills up, it rolls back to zero, which happens about every twenty years. This happened in 1999 and again in 2019. Older receivers that do not adjust for the reset can report a date roughly twenty years in the past.

title: Y2K / Year 2000 problem (two-digit year rollover)
body: Global IT industry; coordinated nationally (e.g. US President's Council on Year 2000 Conversion)
whatChanged: Decades of software stored the year as two decimal digits, so the rollover from 99 to 00 was ambiguous between 2000 and 1900. At midnight 1999-12-31 to 2000-01-01 affected programs could compute negative or nonsensical date intervals and mis-order dated records.
technicalImpact: Mainframe COBOL/PL-I billing, banking, insurance, scheduling and embedded systems risked incorrect interest calculations, mis-sorted records, and crashes. After ~$300B+ of worldwide remediation few major failures occurred, but isolated incidents (parking meters, POS terminals) appeared.

validation.supportingQuote: Until the 1990s many computer programs (especially those written in the early days of computers) were designed to abbreviate four-digit years as two digits... These computers could recognize "98" as "1998" but would be unable to recognize "00" as "2000," perhaps interpreting it to mean 1900. ... After more than a year of international alarm... few major failures occurred in the transition from December 31, 1999, to January 1, 2000.

headline: The year 2000 computer scare
blurb: Many programs stored the year as only two digits, so the jump from 99 to 00 could be read as 1900 instead of 2000.
story: For decades, software saved the year using just two digits to save space. When 1999 turned into 2000, those two digits read as 00, which a program could mistake for 1900. This could make date math come out negative or wrong and put records in the wrong order. After a huge worldwide effort to fix it, very few serious failures happened, though a handful of small ones did.

title: Paul Eggert becomes editor/maintainer of the tz database
body: tz project community; Paul Eggert (UCLA)
whatChanged: Paul Eggert took over as editor and maintainer of the tz code and data on 2005-01-17. Eggert had earlier (1993-10-20) proposed the Area/Location naming convention now used throughout the database. He remains the long-running TZ Coordinator.
technicalImpact: Centralized editorial control under a single coordinator established the cadence of multiple releases per year (2021a, 2022g, 2024b) that OS and language vendors track. Vendor tzdata packages and the JVM tzupdater pull directly from Eggert's releases.

validation.supportingQuote: "Paul Eggert has been its editor and maintainer since 2005" and "Its uniform naming convention for entries in the database, such as America/New_York and Europe/Paris, was designed by Paul Eggert" (with a citation to "Eggert, Paul (20 October 1993). 'proposal for time zone names'").
validation.corrections: date: "2005" (year only; the day-precise 2005-01-17 is unsupported, Eggert became maintainer gradually during 2005, not via a discrete Jan-17 handover). All other fields (responsible body = tz community / Paul Eggert UCLA; 1993-10-20 Area/Location proposal; technical impact) are accurate as written.

headline: New keeper of the time zone records
blurb: Paul Eggert took charge of the shared database that tracks the world's time zone rules.
story: In 2005, Paul Eggert became the editor and maintainer of the widely used time zone database. Years earlier he had proposed the naming style still used for zones today. Under his lead, the database settled into a steady rhythm of several updates each year. Operating systems and programming tools follow these releases to stay current.

title: Energy Policy Act of 2005 extends US DST starting 2007
body: US Congress (109th); signed by President George W. Bush (Public Law 109-58, H.R.6)
whatChanged: Section 110 amended the Uniform Time Act to extend DST by ~four weeks: start moved from first Sunday in April to second Sunday in March, end moved from last Sunday in October to first Sunday in November. Signed 2005-08-08; effective from 2007 (first observance 2007-03-11 to 2007-11-04).
technicalImpact: Caused the 'Year 2007 problem' (DST07): unpatched devices showed local time off by an hour during the new gaps (~2007-03-11 to 2007-04-01 and 2007-10-28 to 2007-11-04), triggering a global patching scramble across Unix/Linux zoneinfo, Java JRE, Windows registry, and embedded devices. Outlook/Exchange required calendar rebasing.

validation.supportingQuote: "The law amended the Uniform Time Act of 1966 by changing the start and end dates of daylight saving time, beginning in 2007. Clocks were set ahead one hour on the second Sunday of March (March 11, 2007) instead of on the first Sunday of April... Energy Policy Act of 2005 (Pub.L. 109-58) ... signed into law by President George W. Bush on August 8, 2005."

headline: United States makes daylight saving longer
blurb: A 2005 law stretched daylight saving time by about four weeks, starting in 2007.
story: In 2005 the United States passed a law to lengthen daylight saving time by roughly four weeks. The change moved the start earlier in spring and the end later in autumn, beginning in 2007. Devices that were not updated showed the wrong time by an hour during the new weeks. This led to a wide rush to patch computers, phones, and calendar software.

title: IANA/Olson tz release 2005m encodes the EPAct 2005 DST rule ahead of 2007
body: tz (Olson/IANA) time zone database project; coordinator Arthur David Olson / Paul Eggert
whatChanged: Released 2005-08-29, tz 2005m updated the US Rule lines to the new second-Sunday-March / first-Sunday-November DST window mandated by EPAct 2005, ~18 months before its 2007-03-11 effective date so downstream OSes and runtimes could ship patches in time.
technicalImpact: The upstream data change every other system inherited. Vendors who shipped tzdata >= 2005m computed 2007 US local time correctly; those on older zoneinfo were off by an hour during extended-DST windows. Defined the patch baseline for the entire 2006-2007 DST remediation.

validation.supportingQuote: Update timezone data files to release 2005m of the zic database. Among other changes, this reflects the recently passed change in USA daylight savings rules.

headline: Time zone update prepares for new daylight saving
blurb: A 2005 release of the time zone database added the new United States daylight saving dates well before they took effect.
story: Soon after the United States changed its daylight saving schedule, the shared time zone database was updated to include the new dates. This update arrived more than a year before the 2007 start, giving everyone time to prepare. Systems that picked up this version handled 2007 correctly. Systems using older data were off by an hour during the new weeks.

title: Leap second #23 inserted at 2005-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS (Bulletin C 30, issued July 2005)
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 32s -> 33s. First leap second after a 7-year hiatus.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second; first since the rise of large-scale Linux/NTP server fleets, surfacing dormant leap-second handling bugs and prompting many ops teams to re-validate NTP configs.
headline: First extra second in seven years
blurb: An extra second was added at the end of 2005, the first since 1998.
story: At the close of 2005, timekeepers added one extra second to the world clock. It was the first such second in about seven years. By this time many servers ran software that had never dealt with an added second before. The event uncovered hidden bugs and pushed many teams to recheck their time setup.

title: Indiana adopts statewide DST and moves counties to Central Time
body: Indiana General Assembly (2005 law) plus US DOT (time-zone boundary final rule, 2006-01-18)
whatChanged: After decades of most counties not observing DST, Indiana law required all 92 counties to observe DST from 2006-04-02. Simultaneously a DOT final rule moved eight counties from Eastern to Central Time; five reverted to Eastern in November 2007.
technicalImpact: Forced creation/realignment of multiple IANA tz Indiana zones (America/Indiana/Indianapolis, /Vincennes, /Knox, /Petersburg, /Tell_City, /Winamac, /Marengo, etc.). Applications keying off a single 'Indiana' assumption broke; correct handling required per-county zone selection.

validation.supportingQuote: DOT is relocating the time zone boundary to move eight Indiana counties (Starke, Pulaski, Knox, Daviess, Martin, Pike, Dubois, and Perry) from Eastern to Central Time Zone... effective April 2, 2006. (Federal Register, "Standard Time Zone Boundary in the State of Indiana," published Jan 20, 2006, 71 FR 3228, Transportation Department / Office of the Secretary)

headline: Indiana finally adopts daylight saving
blurb: Indiana made all its counties observe daylight saving time and shifted some counties to a different time zone.
story: For many years most counties in Indiana did not change their clocks for daylight saving. A 2006 law required all of its counties to start observing it. At the same time, several counties were moved into a neighboring time zone, and a few later moved back. Software that treated the whole state as one time zone broke and had to handle counties separately.

title: Canada matches the extended US DST schedule for 2007
body: Government of Canada plus most provincial/territorial governments
whatChanged: To stay aligned with the US and avoid cross-border disruption, most Canadian provinces/territories legislated through 2005-2006 to adopt the second-Sunday-March to first-Sunday-November DST window beginning in 2007. Saskatchewan (year-round standard time) was unaffected.
technicalImpact: Extended the Year-2007 DST patch scope to all Canadian America/* zones (Toronto, Vancouver, Winnipeg, Halifax, St_Johns); the same tzdata 2005m+ update covered both countries, so unpatched Canadian systems showed identical one-hour offset errors during 2007 gaps.

validation.supportingQuote: Starting in 2007, the United States and Canada will start Daylight Saving Time (DST) on the second Sunday of March and end on the first Sunday of November. From 1987 to 2006, DST began on the first Sunday in April and ended on the last Sunday of October.
validation.corrections: tzRelease should be "2006d" (not "2005m"). tzdata 2005m covered only the US 2007 DST change; Canada's adoption of the new schedule was added in 2006d, per Paul Eggert's 2006-06-27 comment in the IANA tz northamerica file ("For now, assume all of DST-observing Canada will fall into line with the new US DST rules"). The technicalImpact sentence "the same tzdata 2005m+ update covered both countries" is inaccurate, US and Canada landed in separate releases (2005m for US, 2006d for Canada).

headline: Canada matches the new US clock schedule
blurb: Most of Canada changed its daylight saving dates to match the United States for 2007.
story: To stay in step with the United States and avoid confusion at the border, most parts of Canada updated their daylight saving dates for 2007. The new schedule started earlier in spring and ended later in autumn. One province that does not change its clocks was not affected. The same time zone update covered both countries, so unpatched Canadian systems showed the same one hour errors.

title: Venezuela shifts to a half-hour offset, UTC-4:00 to UTC-4:30
body: Government of Venezuela (President Hugo Chavez)
whatChanged: At 03:00 on 2007-12-09 Venezuela set clocks back 30 minutes, moving America/Caracas from UTC-4:00 to UTC-4:30 (legal meridian moved to 67°30' W), reviving the offset used 1912-1965. Stated as an energy/'physiological' measure. The half-hour offset stayed until 2016.
technicalImpact: A rare 30-minute offset broke software assuming whole-hour UTC offsets, hard-coded -4 constants, integer-hour offset storage, and hourly-only zone pickers. The 03:00 -> 02:30 step produced a 30-minute backward discontinuity. Unpatched systems remained 30 minutes off.

validation.supportingQuote: "Venezuela adjusted its time zone from UTC-4 hours to UTC-4:30 hours by turning clocks back 30 minutes at 3:00 AM on December 9, 2007." (corroborated across timeanddate.com and Wikipedia; President Hugo Chavez decreed the change, centered on meridian 67 degrees 30' West, reviving the offset Venezuela used 1912-1964.)

headline: Venezuela sets clocks back half an hour
blurb: In 2007 Venezuela moved its clocks back by thirty minutes instead of a full hour.
story: On a December morning in 2007, Venezuela set its clocks back by thirty minutes. This put the country on an unusual half hour offset that it had used long ago. The half hour setting stayed in place until 2016. Software that assumed time zones always shift by whole hours showed the wrong time, and unpatched systems stayed thirty minutes off.

title: Leap second #24 inserted at 2008-12-31 23:59:60 UTC
body: IERS (Bulletin C 36, issued July 2008)
whatChanged: UTC second 23:59:60 inserted; TAI-UTC 33s -> 34s. First since 2005-12-31.
technicalImpact: Positive leap second. Oracle Cluster Ready Services / Clusterware misbehaved when NTP adjusted time across the leap second, causing cluster node reboots; Oracle recommended running ntpd with slewing (-x). Also the leap second Google's first internal smear experiments targeted.

validation.supportingQuote: Scientists added a second on 31 December 2008, meaning the final countdown required 11 rather than 10 seconds to bring us into the New Year to allow for the time to hit 23:59:60. ... Oracle's Cluster Ready Services software failed to handle this leap second, causing servers ... to reboot automatically after midnight on New Year's Day 2009.

headline: Extra second trips up a database cluster
blurb: An added second at the end of 2008 caused some Oracle cluster servers to restart.
story: At the end of 2008, one extra second was added to the world clock, the first since 2005. When time syncing software adjusted for it, certain Oracle cluster systems misbehaved and rebooted their nodes. Oracle advised changing the time settings to ease the second in gradually. This was also the second that Google used in early tests of its own smoothing approach.

title: IANA tz database codifies POSIX vs 'right' (leap-aware) handling of the UTC regime
body: IANA Time Zone Database (tz) maintainers; theory.html clarified to public domain by Arthur David Olson
whatChanged: The tz project ships two mutually exclusive system-wide configurations: the default POSIX-compatible TZif files exclude leap seconds (each day exactly 86400 seconds), while the separate leap-aware 'right'/zoneinfo-leaps TZif files count every leap second since 1972. theory.html notes POSIX requires leap seconds excluded and that 'many software packages would mishandle leap seconds if they were present.'
technicalImpact: The concrete developer-facing fork created by the 1972 UTC regime: the same timestamp resolves differently depending on configuration, and mixing 'right' and 'posix' zoneinfo on one system corrupts all timestamps. Almost all general-purpose software runs POSIX (leap-ignoring) time and handles leap seconds out-of-band (NTP smearing).

validation.supportingQuote: "leap second support is rarely used directly because POSIX requires leap seconds to be excluded and many software packages would mishandle leap seconds if they were present" ... leap-aware files are "by default installed in a separate directory zoneinfo-leaps" and "the leap-second choice is system-wide" ... "This web page is in the public domain, so clarified as of 2009-05-17 by Arthur David Olson."

headline: Two ways to count time, kept apart
blurb: The time zone database offers two setups, one that ignores added seconds and one that counts them.
story: The time zone project ships two separate setups that cannot be mixed. The default one treats every day as the same length and leaves out the added seconds. The other one counts every added second since 1972. Because of this split, the same moment can read differently depending on which setup a computer uses, and mixing them corrupts timestamps.

title: The 1970/POSIX-epoch accuracy cutoff codified as governing design principle; pre-1970 LMT not reliable
body: tz coordinators (Arthur David Olson, Paul Eggert), documented in IANA tz theory.html
whatChanged: The project formalized in theory.html the principle that zones are partitioned so all clocks within a zone agree about timestamps after 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC; pre-1970 data is best-effort, often from 'unreliable sources, often astrology books', and LMT offsets 'should not be considered meaningful'. theory.html clarified to public domain 2009-05-17. This principle drove the 2021 zone-merging controversy.
technicalImpact: Sets the correctness contract downstream software relies on: timestamps before 1970 may be inaccurate or coalesced when zones merge, so apps must not depend on pre-1970 historical offsets or on distinct zone IDs sharing post-1970 history. Genealogy, historical-finance, and astronomy apps must not trust zoneinfo for pre-1970 accuracy.

validation.supportingQuote: "The tz database attempts to record the history and predicted future of civil time scales... whose clocks all agree about timestamps that occur after the POSIX Epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC)." / "Most of the pre-1970 data entries come from unreliable sources, often astrology books..." / "the tz database's LMT offsets should not be considered meaningful..." / "This web page is in the public domain, so clarified as of 2009-05-17 by Arthur David Olson."

headline: Why old dates are not fully trusted
blurb: The time zone project promises accuracy only for times after the start of 1970.
story: The time zone project set a clear rule about how far back its data can be trusted. Clocks within a zone are guaranteed to agree only for times after the start of 1970. Information from before then is a best effort and sometimes comes from unreliable sources. Apps that deal with history, old finance records, or astronomy should not rely on these older offsets.

title: Y2K10 / 'Y2.01k' 2010 date overflow bugs (BCD/hex misparse)
body: Multiple vendors (SAP, Symantec, Windows Mobile/CE, Bank of Queensland EFTPOS); reported via RISKS Forum
whatChanged: A class of 2010 rollover failures from mishandling the year value: code reading a BCD/decimal year as hexadecimal (0x10 = decimal 16) jumped from 2009 to 2016, while other systems hit hardcoded-year or two-digit assumptions crossing into '10'.
technicalImpact: Symantec Endpoint Protection rejected 2010-dated definitions as expired; Windows Mobile SMS showed messages dated 2016; Bank of Queensland EFTPOS stamped transactions as January 2016 and declined cards; SpamAssassin scored 2010+ mail as spam. Fixes were per-vendor date-parsing patches.

validation.supportingQuote: The main source of problems was confusion between hexadecimal number encoding and binary-coded decimal encodings of numbers. ... BCD encodes the number 10 as 0x10, while hexadecimal encodes the number 10 as 0x0A; 0x10 interpreted as a hexadecimal encoding represents the number 16. [some] mobile phone software incorrectly reported dates of SMSes as 2016 instead of 2010.

headline: The 2010 date glitches
blurb: When 2010 arrived, some systems misread the year and jumped ahead to 2016 or rejected valid dates.
story: As 2010 began, a group of systems mishandled the new year value. Some read the year in the wrong number format and jumped from 2009 to 2016. Security software rejected fresh updates as expired, some phones dated messages as 2016, and certain card machines stamped the wrong year and declined cards. Each affected vendor had to release its own fix for reading dates.

title: PlayStation 3 'ApocalyPS3' BCD leap-year date bug
body: Sony Computer Entertainment (original 'fat' PlayStation 3 RTC firmware)
whatChanged: The original PS3's BCD-based internal clock incorrectly treated 2010 as a leap year, so at midnight UTC it rolled to a non-existent 29 February 2010 instead of 1 March; many consoles then reported the date as 1999-12-31.
technicalImpact: Affected (non-Slim) consoles worldwide showed error 8001050F and could not sign in to PSN, sync trophies, or launch some installed offline games, clock-dependent DRM and date checks failed. The fault self-cleared once internal clocks advanced past the bad date to real 1 March UTC.

validation.supportingQuote: The bug caused the console to treat 2010 as a leap year and so change the date at midnight GMT Sunday to 29 February rather than 1 March. ... Only original PS3 models were affected; slimline versions were unaffected.

headline: PlayStation 3s lost track of the date
blurb: Older PlayStation 3 consoles thought 2010 had a 29 February that does not exist, which knocked them offline.
story: Many older PlayStation 3 consoles wrongly treated 2010 as a leap year. At midnight they tried to roll to 29 February, a day that did not exist that year, and some then showed the date as the last day of 1999. Players around the world could not sign in, sync trophies, or open some games. The problem cleared on its own once the clocks moved past the bad date into real 1 March.

title: Russia abolishes seasonal clock changes, locking onto permanent summer time (Moscow year-round UTC+4)
body: Russian Federation (President Dmitry Medvedev / Federal Law No. 107-FZ), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: After the final spring-forward on 2011-03-27, Medvedev cancelled biannual DST transitions and kept Russia permanently on summer time. Moscow became year-round UTC+4 (no fall-back October 2011), and every Russian zone shifted to permanent DST-offset standard time.
technicalImpact: Software hard-coding Europe/Moscow as UTC+3 with summer DST produced times an hour off for ~8 months a year. DST rules had to be deleted, not just shifted, so libraries carrying old transition rules (older JRE tzdata, un-patched Windows, stale moment-timezone) showed phantom DST changes across 9 zones until updated.

validation.supportingQuote: Search snippet for the canonical article: "the bill entitled 'On the Calculation of Time' was approved by the Duma on April 19, 2011, after Russian president Dmitry Medvedev had already proclaimed the end of 'winter time' in February 2011." Russia "turned its clocks one hour forward for the last time on March 27, 2011, making daylight saving time Russia's new standard time."
validation.linkNote: valid page, blocks automated fetch (HTTP 403 to bots)

headline: Russia stopped changing its clocks
blurb: Russia ended its twice-a-year clock changes and stayed on summer time all year.
story: In 2011 Russia made its final spring clock change and then dropped the seasonal switches for good. The country stayed on summer time year-round, so Moscow kept the same time through every season. Software that still expected the old clock changes showed the wrong time for months until it was updated.

title: Google announces the leap smear: spreading the leap second over a window
body: Google (Public NTP)
whatChanged: Google publicly described its 'leap smear': instead of inserting a literal 23:59:60, Google modified its NTP servers to gradually slew time across a window around the leap second, so no clock shows :60 or steps backward and every day has 86400 seconds. Google had first applied a (cosine) smear for the 2008 leap second.
technicalImpact: Removes the entire class of leap-second software faults (no :60 timestamp, no backward step, no kernel STA_INS handling) for clients reading smeared NTP. Trade-off: smeared time is off from true UTC by up to ~0.5s during the window, so smeared and non-smeared sources must not be mixed. Became the de facto industry pattern.

validation.supportingQuote: "Instead of applying leap seconds to our servers using clock steps, we have 'smeared' the extra second across the hours before and after each leap." ... "Google's 2008 smear was a 20-hour cosine smear before the leap (04:00:00 to 00:00:00)."

headline: Google spread an extra second over a day
blurb: Instead of adding a single odd second, Google nudged its clocks slowly so the change went unnoticed.
story: When an extra second is added to world time, it can confuse computers that do not expect it. Google spread that one second across a long window so its clocks never showed an odd time or jumped backward, and every day still had the usual number of seconds. The trade-off is that during the window Google's time is slightly off from official time, so smeared and unsmeared sources should not be mixed. The approach became a common industry practice.

title: Astrolabe, Inc. v. Olson et al. copyright lawsuit filed
body: Astrolabe, Inc. (astrology software vendor) vs. Arthur David Olson and Paul Eggert; US District Court, District of Massachusetts
whatChanged: Astrolabe sued tz maintainers Olson and Eggert on 2011-09-30, claiming copyright over historical time-zone data the database derived in part from Shanks's 'The American Atlas' / 'The International Atlas'. The claim targeted pre-1970 historical zone information.
technicalImpact: Direct legal threat to maintainers of infrastructure used by virtually all computing time handling. It forced the takedown of the project's mailing list and FTP distribution days later, briefly cutting off the canonical update channel vendors depend on.

validation.supportingQuote: In September of 2011, an astrology software company called Astrolabe filed suit [against Arthur David Olson and Paul Eggert] ... its copyrights were infringed, because the database relies in part on information in an atlas in which Astrolabe claimed to own copyright.

headline: A company sued the time-zone keepers
blurb: A software company sued the people who maintain the world's shared time-zone data over historical records.
story: In 2011 a company called Astrolabe sued the volunteers who maintain the widely used time-zone database. It claimed it owned copyright over some historical time-zone information the database had drawn from old reference books. Because almost all computers rely on this data, the lawsuit was a serious threat to a key piece of shared infrastructure.

title: tz mailing list and FTP distribution site shut down
body: tz project / Arthur David Olson (then at US NIH)
whatChanged: In response to the Astrolabe lawsuit, the database's mailing list and FTP site (hosted at elsie.nci.nih.gov) were taken offline on 2011-10-06, halting official distribution and discussion.
technicalImpact: The authoritative source of tzdata updates went dark with no replacement, creating an acute supply-chain risk: any new government DST/offset change during the outage could not be officially published.

validation.supportingQuote: As a result, on 6 October 2011, the database's mailing list and FTP site were shut down.

headline: The time-zone update channel went dark
blurb: After the lawsuit, the official place to get time-zone updates was taken offline.
story: Soon after the lawsuit, the project's mailing list and download site were shut down. That was the official place where the world got time-zone updates, and it went dark with nothing to replace it. While it was offline, any new clock change made by a government could not be published through the usual channel.

title: ICANN/IANA assumes custodianship of the Time Zone Database
body: ICANN (at the request of the IETF)
whatChanged: On 2011-10-14 ICANN announced it would host and maintain the tz database via IANA, restoring distribution at data.iana.org and agreeing to handle legal matters. Prompted by the Astrolabe lawsuit/takedown and Olson's planned retirement.
technicalImpact: Re-established an authoritative, institutionally backed distribution point (data.iana.org/time-zones) that vendors continue to fetch from. Gave the database an organization able to absorb legal defense, de-risking the single-volunteer dependency.

validation.supportingQuote: ICANN stated it is "willing to deal with any legal matters as they arise," referring to the Astrolabe copyright lawsuit that prompted the withdrawal of the original FTP server.
validation.corrections: Minor wording nuance (not an error): RFC 6557 cites the canonical homepage as http://www.iana.org/time-zones; data.iana.org/time-zones is the data-download path. Both are real and current, so the event's "data.iana.org/time-zones" reference is accurate.

headline: A new home for the time-zone database
blurb: A larger organization stepped in to host and maintain the world's time-zone data.
story: After the lawsuit and the shutdown, the organization ICANN agreed to host and maintain the time-zone database through its IANA group. It restored the official download site and took on the legal side of the work. This gave the database a stable home and a backer able to handle legal trouble, instead of depending on a single volunteer.

title: Samoa (and Tokelau) skip Friday 30 December 2011, jumping from UTC-11 to UTC+13
body: Government of Samoa (and Tokelau), encoded by IANA tz database
whatChanged: At end of Thursday 2011-12-29 Samoa moved its standard offset from UTC-11 to UTC+13 (DST effectively UTC+14), placing it west of the International Date Line to align trading days with Australia/New Zealand. Friday 2011-12-30 was skipped entirely and never existed locally. Tokelau (Pacific/Fakaofo) made the same jump simultaneously.
technicalImpact: A 24-hour discontinuity with no local midnight on 2011-12-30 broke any system iterating calendar days, computing durations, or storing timestamps assuming every date exists. Pacific/Apia gained a +24h discontinuity, the largest single-day local-time jump ever encoded. The textbook case for converting through UTC and never assuming every calendar date exists locally.

validation.supportingQuote: In 2011, Samoa changed its time zone from UTC-11 to UTC+13 by shifting the date line to the west and removing December 30, 2011 from the calendar. The change occurred at midnight on December 29, 2011, taking the Pacific island nation straight into December 31, 2011.

headline: Samoa skipped a day
blurb: Samoa jumped across the date line, so Friday 30 December 2011 never happened there.
story: At the end of one Thursday in December 2011, Samoa moved to the other side of the international date line to line up its working week with Australia and New Zealand. As a result the following Friday simply did not exist there and went straight to Saturday. The nearby islands of Tokelau made the same jump at the same time. Any software that assumed every calendar date exists could break on that missing day.

title: RFC 6557 (BCP 175): Procedures for Maintaining the Time Zone Database
body: IETF; authors Eliot Lear (Cisco) and Paul Eggert (UCLA); published as BCP 175
whatChanged: Published February 2012, RFC 6557 formalized tz governance after the lawsuit and ICANN takeover: it defines the TZ Coordinator role, the tz@iana.org mailing list, update/appeal procedures, IANA's duty to host the repository and hold the signing key, and the public-domain status.
technicalImpact: Turned an ad-hoc single-volunteer process into a documented, succession-proof procedure, assuring vendors updates would continue through a defined channel with cryptographically signed releases.

validation.supportingQuote: RFC 6557, "Procedures for Maintaining the Time Zone Database," BCP 175, by Eliot Lear (Cisco Systems GmbH) and Paul Eggert (UCLA), February 2012. Defines the TZ Coordinator role, the tz@iana.org mailing list, IANA maintenance of a repository with cryptographic signing, and that the TZ database "is in the public domain, and is intended to remain in the public domain."

headline: Clear rules for running the time-zone database
blurb: A published standard set out how the world's time-zone database should be maintained.
story: After the lawsuit and the move to a new host, a formal document laid out how the time-zone database would be run. It defined who coordinates it, how updates are made and appealed, where it is stored, and that it stays free for anyone to use. This turned a loose, one-person process into a documented system that could survive changes in the people involved.

title: Astrolabe v. Olson lawsuit voluntarily dismissed
body: Astrolabe, Inc.; defendants represented by the Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF)
whatChanged: On 2012-02-22 Astrolabe voluntarily moved to dismiss the suit without having served the defendants, after EFF intervened and sought Rule 11 sanctions. Astrolabe apologized and entered a covenant not to sue.
technicalImpact: Removed the legal cloud over redistributing tzdata, confirming the project could continue using historical sources and ship freely under public-domain terms. Stabilized the legal footing downstream OS/language vendors rely on.

validation.supportingQuote: The astrology software company that filed the lawsuit, Astrolabe, has also apologized and agreed to a 'covenant not to sue' going forward.

headline: The time-zone lawsuit was dropped
blurb: The company dropped its lawsuit against the time-zone maintainers and promised not to sue again.
story: In early 2012 the company that had sued the time-zone maintainers withdrew its case, after a digital rights group stepped in to defend them. The company apologized and agreed not to bring the claim again. This lifted the legal cloud and confirmed the project could keep using historical sources and share its data freely.

title: Windows Azure 2012 leap-day certificate date bug outage
body: Microsoft (Windows Azure / Azure Fabric Guest Agent)
whatChanged: The Guest Agent computed a transfer certificate's valid-to date by adding 1 to the current year. On 2012-02-29 that produced the non-existent 2013-02-29, so certificate creation failed.
technicalImpact: From 00:00 UTC 2012-02-29 new VM provisioning failed; hosts retried, declared hardware faults and migrated workloads, cascading into a multi-cluster outage. Recovery took ~13h 23m for most clusters; a botched rollback on seven clusters extended the disruption. Microsoft issued service credits.

validation.supportingQuote: The Guest Agent (GA) calculated the valid-to date by simply taking the current date and adding one to its year, meaning that any GA that tried to create a transfer certificate on leap day set a valid-to date of February 29, 2013, an invalid date that caused the certificate creation to fail.

headline: A leap day broke a cloud service
blurb: On 29 February 2012, a date mistake took down part of Microsoft's cloud.
story: Microsoft's cloud service worked out a security date by simply adding one to the current year. On 29 February 2012 that produced 29 February 2013, a date that does not exist, and the step failed. New machines could not be set up, and the trouble spread into a wider outage that took many hours to fix. Microsoft gave affected customers service credits.

title: Red Hat Enterprise Linux leap-second guidance: futex CPU spike (RHEL 6) and early-hrtimer (RHEL 7)
body: Red Hat (vendor advisory RHBA-2012:1199 and KB)
whatChanged: Red Hat documented two distinct leap-second kernel defects: on RHEL 6 the leap second caused futex-heavy applications to consume large amounts of CPU; on RHEL 7 hrtimers could fire early. Also noted a spurious leap-second notification erroneously issued by some North American NTP servers for 2012-08-31.
technicalImpact: Production guidance: disable kernel leap-second insertion and slew instead (stop ntpd, step the clock, restart) or apply patched kernels. Java/MySQL/Firefox-class workloads were the canonical victims of the futex CPU spike.

validation.supportingQuote: After the leap second has been inserted futex heavy applications began consuming a large amount of CPU... Hrtimers may fire early when the leap second is inserted... An additional leap second notification was erroneously issued by some NTP servers within the North America region for midnight UTC on August 31, 2012.

headline: Red Hat warned about an extra second
blurb: Red Hat told customers how to avoid software problems caused by an added second.
story: Red Hat found that adding an extra second to world time could cause problems on its Linux systems, with some software using far too much processor power and some timers firing too early. It advised customers to turn off the automatic second insertion and adjust the clock gently instead, or to apply fixed software. Common programs like Java and MySQL were the usual victims.

title: Leap second #25 (2012-06-30) triggers Linux kernel hrtimer livelock, crashing major sites
body: IERS (Bulletin C 43) inserted the leap second; root-cause bug in the Linux kernel timekeeping/hrtimer subsystem
whatChanged: A positive leap second was inserted at 2012-06-30 23:59:60 UTC (TAI-UTC 34s -> 35s). The kernel applied the leap second but failed to call clock_was_set() to notify the hrtimer subsystem, so CLOCK_REALTIME hrtimers were not reprogrammed: timers fired early/immediately, producing a livelock. Bug present since ~2.6.22.
technicalImpact: futex-heavy and timer-heavy processes (Java/JVM, MySQL, Firefox) spun at 100% CPU.
