Reddit was down ~1.5h; Mozilla (Hadoop/ElasticSearch), LinkedIn, Yelp, Foursquare, StumbleUpon were hit; the Amadeus Altea airline reservation system (Qantas, Virgin Australia) failed, forcing manual check-in. Workaround: 'date -s now'. Fixed by John Stultz's patch adding clock_was_set() (commit bb88e92477def647976cd3d6964af98beceba900).

validation.supportingQuote: "It is believed the leap second causes the Linux kernel to livelock when the system attempts to adjust the time and date of the computer." (headline: "Leap second bug cripples Linux servers at airlines, Reddit, LinkedIn"; affected: Qantas and Virgin Australia, plus servers run by Mozilla, StumbleUpon, Yelp, FourSquare, Reddit and LinkedIn; leap second inserted "midnight on Saturday, 30 June").

headline: An extra second crashed major websites
blurb: A one-second adjustment in 2012 set off a Linux bug that froze big sites.
story: An extra second was added to world time in mid-2012, but a long-standing Linux bug failed to tell part of the system about it. Timers then fired too early and locked up, so many programs spun at full processor load. Reddit went down for about an hour and a half, several other well-known sites were hit, and an airline reservation system failed, forcing manual check-in. A quick fix was to reset the clock, and a proper patch followed.

title: tz database removes the Saudi Arabia solar-time zones (solar87, solar88, solar89)
body: IANA / tz database project (Paul Eggert et al.)
whatChanged: The tz distribution had shipped solar87/88/89, experimental zones encoding apparent solar time at Riyadh, where each day had a different offset (~UTC+3:07 nominal) driven by the equation of time, with hundreds of daily Rule lines. Release 2013i (2013-12-17) stopped distributing them as 'a negative experiment'.
technicalImpact: These zones nearly exhausted the TZif format's hard limit (time-type indices stored as unsigned char, max 256 distinct types), forcing 5-second rounding of every day's offset, a concrete demonstration of zoneinfo's internal capacity limits. Software referencing 'Asia/Riyadh87' had to fall back to Asia/Riyadh.

validation.supportingQuote: The files solar87, solar88, and solar89 are no longer distributed. They were a negative experiment, that is, a demonstration that tz data can represent solar time only with some difficulty and error.
validation.corrections: No core correction needed. Minor nuance: release/commit date = 2013-12-17 (matches event); tz-announce email date = 2013-12-19. The removal RATIONALE in the event's technicalImpact (256-type limit exhaustion forcing 5-second rounding) is NOT the official stated reason, official reason was "negative experiment" causing confusion. The 256-type unsigned-char limit and apparent-solar/equation-of-time nature are independently true but were not cited as the cause of removal.

headline: Saudi solar-time zones were removed
blurb: An experiment that tracked the sun's exact time over Riyadh was dropped from the time-zone data.
story: The time-zone data once included experimental entries that followed the sun's actual position over Riyadh, giving every single day a slightly different time. They needed hundreds of rules a day and nearly hit a hard limit in the data format. In late 2013 the project removed them, calling the attempt a failed experiment. Anything still pointing to those entries had to fall back to the normal Saudi zone.

title: Antarctica/Troll added: the only zone that swings UTC+0 <-> UTC+2 with a two-hour DST jump
body: IANA / tz database project (data from Norwegian Polar Institute contributors)
whatChanged: Release 2014b (2014-03-24) added Antarctica/Troll for Norway's Troll research station. It uses UTC+0 from October to March and UTC+2 (matching CEST) from March to October, a two-hour shift, transitioning at CET/CEST dates to stay aligned with mainland Norway's logistics flights.
technicalImpact: The only zone where SAVE = 2:00, so code assuming DST adds at most one hour mis-handles Troll's spring-forward (clocks jump 01:00->03:00, a two-hour gap) and fall-back (two-hour ambiguous window). The definitive test case for libraries to validate they read the actual SAVE value and correctly size skipped/repeated intervals.

validation.supportingQuote: Rule Troll 2005 max - Mar lastSun 1:00u 2:00 +02 ... From Paul Eggert (2014-03-21): The CET-switching Troll rules require zic from tz 2014b or later
validation.corrections: Replace "It uses UTC+0 in the austral winter and UTC+2 (matching CEST) in the austral summer" with "It uses UTC+0 in the austral summer (Oct-March) and UTC+2 (matching CEST) in the austral winter (the dark March-October season with no flights, following mainland Norway's summer time)." The two-hour spring-forward occurs on the last Sunday of March (austral autumn/winter onset), fall-back on the last Sunday of October.

headline: A station that shifts its clock two hours
blurb: An Antarctic research station was added with a rare two-hour seasonal clock change.
story: In 2014 a new time zone was added for Norway's Troll research station in Antarctica. To stay in step with flights to and from mainland Norway, it shifts its clock by a full two hours between seasons rather than the usual one. That makes it the only zone with a two-hour seasonal jump, which trips up software that assumes the change is always one hour.

title: Egypt re-introduces DST on short notice in 2014 with mid-period Ramadan suspensions
body: Arab Republic of Egypt (interim government / Cabinet decision May 2014), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: After abolishing DST in 2011, Egypt's interim government reinstated it with ~a week's notice: DST started 2014-05-15, then clocks fell back for Ramadan (2014-06-26 to 2014-07-31) and sprang forward again afterward, producing four offset transitions in a single summer.
technicalImpact: The week-of notice outran normal tzdata/OS update cycles; Microsoft shipped emergency 'Easy fix' hotfixes, IBM issued APAR IV61426, and moment-timezone was re-patched. The four-transition Ramadan pattern broke code assuming at most two DST transitions per year, and the religiously-timed dates defeated future-date guessing. Devices disagreed by an hour around each transition until patched (tzdata 2014c, refined 2014e).

validation.supportingQuote: Egypt's 2014 Ramadan-based transitions are June 26 and July 31 at 24:00. Egypt temporarily switches to standard time at midnight the last Thursday before Ramadan, then returns to DST at midnight the first Friday after Ramadan.

headline: Egypt changed clocks four times in one summer
blurb: Egypt brought back daylight saving with about a week of notice, then paused it for Ramadan, so clocks shifted four times that summer.
story: In 2014 Egypt decided to use daylight saving time again after dropping it in 2011. The notice was very short, only about a week. Clocks then moved back for the month of Ramadan and forward again afterward, so the time changed four times in one summer. Phones and computers that had not been updated showed the wrong time by an hour around each change.

title: Russia reverts to permanent standard time, cutting most zones by an hour and re-splitting its zone map
body: Russian Federation (Federal Law No. 248-FZ, signed by President Putin), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: On 2014-10-26 at 02:00 local most Russian zones subtracted one hour and froze on permanent standard time (no DST). Moscow moved from UTC+4 to year-round UTC+3. The law also re-drew the zone map: some oblasts subtracted two hours while others kept their offset, adding new fixed zones.
technicalImpact: A one-time backward offset change with no recurring rule meant every Russian timestamp after 2014-10-26 from an un-updated system was an hour ahead. Because regions changed by different amounts (0, 1, or 2 hours) and some split into new zones (Asia/Chita, Asia/Srednekolymsk), simple country-wide patches were wrong; libraries needed the full tzdata 2014f zone reshaping across 11 zones.

validation.supportingQuote: "Most Russians moved to permanent Standard Time, or 'winter time', by setting their clocks 1 hour back on October 26, 2014... The bill also introduced 2 new time zones... increasing the number of time zones in Russia from 9 to 11." President Vladimir Putin signed the law on July 22, 2014.
validation.corrections: In whatChanged/technicalImpact, replace the new-zone example 'Europe/Samara' with 'Asia/Chita'. Correct phrasing: "some split into new zones (Asia/Chita, Asia/Srednekolymsk)". Europe/Samara was not new in 2014f and did not shift on 2014-10-26.

headline: Russia moved most clocks back and stopped daylight saving
blurb: Russia set most of its regions to a permanent winter time, with some areas changing more than others.
story: On a single day in 2014 most of Russia moved its clocks back and stopped changing them for the seasons. Moscow settled on a fixed year-round time. Different regions changed by different amounts, and a few were placed into new time zones. Computers that were not updated showed Russian times an hour off afterward.

title: Boeing 787 GCU 248-day software counter overflow
body: U.S. FAA (Airworthiness Directive 2015-09-07); Boeing
whatChanged: A software counter inside the 787's Generator Control Units increments at a fixed rate and overflows after 248 days of continuous power (consistent with a signed 32-bit counter ticking ~every 10 ms). On overflow each affected GCU enters fail-safe mode.
technicalImpact: If all four main GCUs are powered up simultaneously, after 248 continuous days they all enter fail-safe at once, causing loss of all AC electrical power regardless of flight phase. The FAA's AD (effective 2015-05-01) mandated a periodic power-cycle pending a Boeing software fix.

validation.supportingQuote: A Model 787 airplane that has been powered continuously for 248 days can lose all alternating current (AC) electrical power due to the generator control units (GCUs) simultaneously going into failsafe mode... caused by a software counter internal to the GCUs that will overflow after 248 days of continuous power. (FAA AD 2015-09-07, effective May 1, 2015)

headline: Boeing 787 power units failed after 248 days on
blurb: A counter inside the 787's power control units overflowed after 248 days of nonstop power and put them into a safe shutdown mode.
story: The Boeing 787 has units that control its electrical generators. A counter inside them runs up while the power stays on and overflows after 248 days without a break. When that happens the units switch into a protective shutdown. If all four ran nonstop at once, they could shut down together and cut the plane's main electrical power, so regulators required crews to power the system off and on at regular intervals until a software fix was ready.

title: AWS adopts a 24-hour leap smear for the 2015 leap second
body: Amazon Web Services (vendor mitigation)
whatChanged: AWS announced it would not insert a literal leap second; instead each AWS clock-second would be lengthened by 1/86400 across a 24-hour window (12:00 UTC 2015-06-30 to 12:00 UTC 2015-07-01), so AWS clocks fall behind, never show :60, and re-converge with UTC.
technicalImpact: AWS-hosted systems (EC2, Management Console, billing, CloudFront/CloudTrail timestamps) avoid the :60 / backward-step faults. AWS time can differ from external UTC by up to ~0.5s during the window, so cross-checking against non-smeared sources shows drift.

validation.supportingQuote: Starting at 12:00:00 PM on June 30th, 2015, we will slow down AWS clocks by 1/86400. Every second on AWS clocks will take 1+1/86400 seconds of 'real' time, until 12:00:00 PM on July 1st, 2015 ... You will not see a ':60' second in these places.

headline: Amazon stretched a day to absorb a leap second
blurb: Amazon spread one extra second across a full day so its clocks never had to show an unusual sixty-first second.
story: In 2015 the world's clocks were due to add one extra second. Instead of adding it all at once, Amazon made each second of a 24-hour window very slightly longer. That let its clocks take in the extra second gradually and stay smooth, never showing an odd time. During that window Amazon's time could differ from official time by up to about half a second.

title: Leap second #26 (2015-06-30): GNSS receivers apply it early, causing -1s offsets
body: IERS (Bulletin C 49); GNSS receiver vendors (faulty firmware)
whatChanged: A positive leap second was inserted at 2015-06-30 23:59:60 UTC (TAI-UTC 35s -> 36s), first since 2012; encoded in tzdb 2015a. Several GNSS receiver models (TrueTime XL-AK, unpatched Trimble Lassen SQ/iQ) applied the announced leap second as soon as it appeared in the GPS almanac rather than at the scheduled epoch, retarding their time by one second early.
technicalImpact: Faulty receivers fed a -1s offset into NTP servers, radios, computer clocks and seismic/data-logging equipment (USGS NCSN observed it in Jan 2015; US Coast Guard NAVCEN issued an alert). Downstream NTP clients inherited the wrong time. Prompted broad smear adoption (AWS, Google) and post-2012 kernel hardening validation.

validation.supportingQuote: 3644697600	36	# 1 Jul 2015, preceded by the 2012 entry at DTAI 35, confirming TAI-UTC 35s -> 36s. File header: "Updated through IERS Bulletin C (https://hpiers.obspm.fr/iers/bul/bulc/bulletinc.dat)".

headline: A leap second tripped up some GPS receivers
blurb: An extra second was added to world time in 2015, and some GPS receivers applied it too early and ran a second slow.
story: In mid 2015 one extra second was added to official world time, the first such addition in three years. Some GPS receiver models applied the change before the scheduled moment and ended up a second behind. Those faulty receivers passed the wrong time to other equipment that relied on them. The trouble pushed more companies to smooth the change over time instead of adding the second all at once.

title: North Korea adopts Pyongyang Time (UTC+8:30), splitting from the Korean peninsula
body: Government of the DPRK; encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: On 5 Aug 2015 the DPRK announced that on 15 Aug 2015 (70th anniversary of liberation) it would move standard time back 30 minutes from UTC+9 to UTC+8:30, naming it 'Pyongyang Time' (PYT, keeping the abbreviation 'KST'), framed as erasing a legacy of Japanese imperialism. First 30 minutes behind South Korea/Japan since 1912.
technicalImpact: Created a new half-hour offset for Asia/Pyongyang diverging from Asia/Seoul. Systems not patched to tz 2015f computed North Korean time half an hour wrong; with only 10 days notice, devices showed +9 until updated. Reusing 'KST' for +8:30 confused parsers expecting KST=+9.

validation.supportingQuote: North Korea switches to +0830 on 2015-08-15. (Thanks to Steffen Thorsen.)

headline: North Korea set its own clock half an hour back
blurb: North Korea moved its clocks back thirty minutes, putting the country half an hour behind its neighbors.
story: In 2015 North Korea announced that it would move its clocks back by thirty minutes, taking effect on an anniversary date about ten days later. It called the result Pyongyang Time. This left the country half an hour behind South Korea and Japan. Systems that had not been updated kept showing the old time until they caught up.

title: Turkey postpones the 2015 DST end, but auto-updating clocks fell back anyway
body: Republic of Turkey (government decree for the Nov 1 general election), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: To keep polling stations in daylight for the 2015-11-01 election, Turkey moved its DST-end from the normal last Sunday of October (2015-10-25) to 2015-11-08, extending the summer offset (+03) by two weeks.
technicalImpact: tzdata 2015g (2015-10-01) carried the fix, but devices that hadn't updated fell back at 03:00 on 2015-10-25 regardless, so phones, PCs, and servers across Turkey disagreed by an hour for two weeks. The hashtag #saatkac trended; a canonical example of why late, politically-driven tz changes break automatic clocks.

validation.supportingQuote: Due to general elections, Turkey delayed the end of DST 2015 by two weeks. Clocks were turned back one hour from 4:00 to 3:00 local time on Sunday, November 8, 2015... The change of the DST date was due to Turkey's general election on November 1, and the delay ensured daylight during the opening hours of the polling stations.
validation.linkNote: valid page, blocks automated fetch (HTTP 403 to bots)

headline: Turkey delayed a clock change but some clocks switched anyway
blurb: Turkey put off the end of daylight saving by two weeks, yet many devices fell back on the old date and showed the wrong time.
story: In 2015 Turkey delayed the end of daylight saving by two weeks so polling places would stay in daylight for an election. Updated systems handled the new date, but many phones, computers, and servers that had not been updated changed their clocks on the original date anyway. For about two weeks clocks across Turkey disagreed by an hour. It became a well known example of how late changes confuse automatic clocks.

title: ITU WRC-15 defers leap-second decision, retains leap seconds
body: ITU-R, World Radiocommunication Conference 2015 (WRC-15), Geneva
whatChanged: After years of debate over suppressing the leap second and moving Recommendation ITU-R TF.460 toward a continuous reference time scale, WRC-15 declined to decide, retaining leap seconds and deferring further study toward WRC-23. This impasse pushed the question to the BIPM/CGPM track.
technicalImpact: Prolonged a decade of uncertainty for timekeeping vendors, GNSS, and OS/NTP maintainers, who kept supporting leap-second insertion indefinitely, leaving the 2012/2017-class bugs live and pushing vendors toward unilateral smearing.

validation.supportingQuote: "UTC shall continue to be applied as described in Recommendation ITU-R TF.460-6 and as maintained by the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM)." The conference deferred a decision on suppressing the leap second, calling for further studies before a 2023 conference.

headline: A 2015 meeting kept leap seconds and put off deciding
blurb: An international conference chose not to decide on ending the leap second, so the practice continued.
story: For years officials had debated whether to stop adding leap seconds to world time. At a 2015 conference they declined to make a decision and kept the leap second in place, pushing the question to later study. This left timekeeping vendors and software maintainers facing continued uncertainty. They had to keep supporting leap seconds, and some companies smoothed the changes on their own.

title: iOS 'January 1, 1970' epoch underflow bricking bug
body: Apple (64-bit iOS devices, iOS 8/9; fixed in iOS 9.3)
whatChanged: Setting a 64-bit iOS device's clock to 1970-01-01 set Unix time to 0; subsequent attempts to render an earlier timestamp produced a negative (underflowed) time value that triggered a segmentation fault in time-zone/locale initialization during boot.
technicalImpact: Affected iPhones/iPads/iPods entered a permanent reboot loop (effectively bricked) and could not be recovered normally; the date could also be pushed remotely via malicious NTP over open Wi-Fi. Apple fixed it in iOS 9.3 by rejecting/clamping out-of-range epoch-zero and pre-epoch timestamps.

validation.supportingQuote: Manually changing the date to May 1970 or earlier can prevent your iOS device from turning on after a restart. An upcoming software update will prevent this issue from affecting iOS devices.

headline: Setting an iPhone to 1970 could break it
blurb: Setting a 64-bit Apple device's date to January 1, 1970 could send it into an endless restart loop.
story: On 64-bit iPhones and iPads, setting the date to the very start of 1970 confused the software during startup. The device could fall into a permanent restart loop and become very hard to recover. The bad date could even be pushed to a device over an open Wi-Fi network. Apple fixed it in a later software update by rejecting these out-of-range dates.

title: RFC 7808: Time Zone Data Distribution Service (TZDIST)
body: IETF TZDIST working group (originating in CalConnect); authors M. Douglass and C. Daboo
whatChanged: Published March 2016, RFC 7808 defined an HTTP/JSON service for reliable, secure, fast delivery of time-zone data and leap-second rules to clients, with conditional/incremental requests so clients sync only changed zones. Companion RFC 7809 let CalDAV clients reference time zones by ID.
technicalImpact: Let calendaring/scheduling apps and OSes pull tz updates over the network independently of OS/vendor release cycles, closing the gap where a client couldn't get a fresh DST rule until its platform shipped a tzdata package. Reduced wrong-time bugs from stale embedded VTIMEZONE definitions.

validation.supportingQuote: Title: "Time Zone Data Distribution Service"; Publication Date: March 2016; Authors: M. Douglass and C. Daboo. The service uses HTTP with a JSON-based API to enable efficient delivery of time zone updates.

headline: A new standard for sending time-zone updates online
blurb: A 2016 standard let apps and systems download fresh time-zone rules over the internet on their own.
story: In 2016 a new technical standard described a way to deliver time-zone and leap-second rules to devices over the internet. Apps could request only the parts that had changed, keeping updates small. This let calendar apps and operating systems get fresh rules without waiting for a full software release. It helped reduce wrong-time problems caused by outdated time-zone data.

title: Venezuela reverts the half-hour offset, UTC-4:30 back to UTC-4:00
body: Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela (President Nicolas Maduro), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: At 02:30 on 2016-05-01 Venezuela advanced clocks 30 minutes, returning America/Caracas from UTC-4:30 to UTC-4:00 to save electricity during an El Nino hydropower crisis, reversing the 2007 change.
technicalImpact: Announced with ~a week's notice, forcing out-of-cycle tzdata (2016a, refined 2016d) and vendor hotfixes. Systems still on -4:30 ran 30 minutes ahead; embedded devices, POS terminals, and un-patched servers stayed wrong for weeks. Stored future timestamps cached under -4:30 became wrong, a classic 'tz update invalidates persisted local times' hazard.

validation.supportingQuote: Venezuela's government announced they would advance their clocks forward by 30 minutes on May 1st, 2016 at 02:30 AM... changed Venezuela's standard time zone offset from UTC -04:30 to UTC -04:00. President Nicolas Maduro announced the half-hour change... to reduce electricity demand in the early evenings.

headline: Venezuela moved its clocks forward half an hour
blurb: Venezuela shifted its clocks ahead by thirty minutes to save power, undoing an earlier change.
story: In 2016 Venezuela moved its clocks forward by thirty minutes to help save electricity during a power shortage. This undid a change it had made years earlier. The announcement came with only about a week of notice, so software had to be patched quickly. Devices that were not updated stayed half an hour off for weeks, and some saved future appointments ended up wrong.

title: Egypt cancels reinstated DST three days before it was to begin
body: Government of Egypt (Cabinet/Parliament); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Egypt announced in April 2016 it would reintroduce DST starting 7 Jul 2016 (after Ramadan). On 4-5 Jul 2016, three days before the switch, the Cabinet reversed course and cancelled DST entirely, keeping Africa/Cairo on UTC+2.
technicalImpact: A textbook last-minute timezone failure. IANA had shipped tz 2016e with the 7 Jul start and Microsoft a June update, so within 96 hours billions of devices couldn't be re-patched. iPhones/Samsung phones auto-advanced on 7-8 Jul, Google Calendar/Search showed wrong times, and Emirates flight 926 departed Cairo an hour early. IANA published 2016f on 5 Jul; Windows fix KB3177723 only arrived 18 Aug 2016.

validation.supportingQuote: Release 2016f - 2016-07-05 ... The Egyptian government changed its mind on short notice, and Africa/Cairo will not introduce DST starting 2016-07-07 after all.

headline: Egypt canceled a clock change three days before it
blurb: Egypt called off a planned return to daylight saving just three days before it was due to start.
story: In 2016 Egypt said it would bring back daylight saving in early July. Three days before the switch, the government reversed the plan and canceled it. By then the change had already been built into software worldwide, and there was no time to undo it everywhere. Many phones advanced an hour anyway, some calendars showed wrong times, and one flight even left Cairo an hour early.

title: Turkey cancels the autumn 2016 fall-back and adopts permanent UTC+3
body: Republic of Turkey (Council of Ministers decree, Resmi Gazete 2016-09-08), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Turkey announced it would skip the scheduled 2016-10-30 fall-back and stay permanently on the summer offset, switching Europe/Istanbul from EET/EEST (+02/+03) to a permanent +03 effective 2016-09-07. Turkey Time became year-round UTC+3 with no further DST.
technicalImpact: Weeks before the planned transition, forcing an out-of-cycle tzdata release (2016g) and emergency vendor patches (Windows hotfix, Joda-Time #403, Debian #838781). Unpatched systems performed the cancelled fall-back, leaving Turkey an hour behind; mixed-version fleets showed two different Turkish times simultaneously.

validation.supportingQuote: Turkey switched from EET/EEST (+02/+03) to permanent +03, effective 2016-09-07.

headline: Turkey kept summer time all year
blurb: Turkey skipped its autumn clock change and stayed on summer time permanently.
story: In 2016 Turkey announced it would not turn its clocks back that autumn and would keep summer time all year. The change took effect weeks before the planned switch. Software makers had to release emergency updates to keep up. Systems that were not updated still turned their clocks back, leaving some devices an hour behind and showing two different Turkish times at once.

title: Google standardizes the public 24-hour noon-to-noon linear leap smear
body: Google (Public NTP); subsequently adopted by AWS and others as a de-facto standard
whatChanged: Google formalized a recommended leap-smear: spread the leap second linearly over 24 hours centered on the event (noon-to-noon UTC), so no 23:59:60 ever appears on client clocks. The public time.google.com service and documented standard launched 2017; Google previously used 20-hour (2012/2015/2016) and 20-hour cosine (2008) windows.
technicalImpact: Clocks deviate up to ~0.5s from UTC during the smear but never step or repeat a second, sidestepping the 2012/2017-class bugs. Frequency change ~11.6 ppm, within quartz tolerance and well under NTP's 500 ppm slew cap. Provides a precise, reproducible definition so multiple providers (Google, AWS, Meta) interoperate.

validation.supportingQuote: Google's official time service page documents a "24-hour linear smear from noon to noon UTC" as the recommended standard, notes it "previously used a 20-hour smear duration" (and a 2008 "20-hour cosine smear"), cites the ~11.6 ppm frequency change, and confirms AWS adopted the same noon-to-noon approach.
validation.corrections: date: change "2017-08-01" to "2016-11-30" (public launch of Google Public NTP, time.google.com) or, if dating the 24-hour standard specifically, to "2016-12" (proposed on public-ntp-discuss, Dec 2016) with first intended use at leap second #38 (~2018). The body's claim that "the documented standard launched 2017" is unsupported and should be removed/corrected; nothing leap-related launched in 2017 (no 2017 leap second). Everything else (body, responsible party, technical impact) is accurate.

headline: Google set a standard way to absorb leap seconds
blurb: Google defined a method that spreads a leap second smoothly over a full day so clocks never show an odd extra second.
story: Google created a clear, recommended way to handle the extra leap second added to world time. The method spreads the second evenly across a full day, centered on the event, so clocks never display an unusual time. Clocks drift up to about half a second during this window but never jump or repeat. Because the method is precise and shared, different companies can use it and stay in step with each other.

title: Leap second #27 (most recent, 2016-12-31): Cloudflare RRDNS Go service panics on negative time delta
body: IERS (Bulletin C 52) inserted the leap second; bug in Cloudflare's Go-based RRDNS
whatChanged: A positive leap second was inserted at 2016-12-31 23:59:60 UTC (TAI-UTC 36s -> 37s; encoded in tzdb 2016g). The 27th and most recent leap second. In Cloudflare's RRDNS, an elapsed-time value computed from two time.Now() readings went negative across the leap second; that value was passed to rand.Int63n(), which panics on a negative argument.
technicalImpact: RRDNS instances panicked servicing DNS for CNAME records, returning SERVFAIL: ~0.2% of DNS queries and <1% of HTTP requests errored across Cloudflare's PoPs, patched in ~90 minutes. Fix broadened the upstream-RTT check from '== 0' to '<= 0'. A classic monotonic-vs-wall-clock confusion (Go's time.Now() was not monotonic before Go 1.9). DTAI has remained 37s ever since.

validation.supportingQuote: "rand.Int63n promptly panics if its argument is negative. That's where the RRDNS panics were coming from." ... "The problem only affected customers who use CNAME DNS records with Cloudflare" ... "at peak approximately 0.2% of DNS queries to Cloudflare were affected."

headline: An extra second crashed Cloudflare
blurb: A leap second at the end of 2016 made part of Cloudflare's system fail.
story: On the last day of 2016, an extra second was added to the world clock. A piece of Cloudflare's software measured time across that moment and got a negative result, which it could not handle, so it crashed. A small share of web requests failed for about 90 minutes before the company fixed it.

title: Chile's Magallanes region stops observing DST, going permanent UTC-3 (new zone created)
body: Government of Chile (President Michelle Bachelet); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Chile cancelled the scheduled 14 May 2017 fall-back for its southernmost Magallanes (and Chilean Antarctic) region, keeping it on permanent UTC-3 while the rest of the country continued switching between -04 and -03. Bachelet signed the enabling law 4 Dec 2017.
technicalImpact: Forced the IANA tz database to split Chile into a new zone, America/Punta_Arenas, separate from America/Santiago. Systems treating all mainland Chile as one zone computed Magallanes time wrong by an hour for part of the year. Chile is a recurring tz headache, repeatedly changing DST dates on short notice.

validation.supportingQuote: Magallanes is currently observing DST, and the transition back to standard time, scheduled for 14 May, 2017, has been cancelled. ... Magallanes is 3 hours behind UTC.
validation.corrections: tzRelease should be "2017a" (released 2017-02-28), not "2016k". Quote from IANA tz-announce 2017a: "Chile's Region of Magallanes moves from -04/-03 to -03 year-round. Its clocks diverge from America/Santiago starting 2017-05-13 at 23:00, hiving off a new zone America/Punta_Arenas."

headline: Chile's far south stopped changing clocks
blurb: Chile's southernmost region stopped switching its clocks for summer and winter.
story: In 2017 Chile decided that its far southern Magallanes region would stop changing its clocks twice a year. The rest of the country kept switching, so the two areas no longer matched for part of the year. To track this, the main time database created a separate zone for the region.

title: North Korea reverts to UTC+9 (KST) to realign with South Korea
body: Government of the DPRK (Kim Jong-un); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Ahead of the April 2018 inter-Korean summit, Kim Jong-un ordered clocks moved forward 30 minutes, abolishing Pyongyang Time and returning Asia/Pyongyang to UTC+9. The transition actually occurred at 23:30 on 2018-05-04 (clocks jumped to 00:00), not at 00:00 on 2018-05-05.
technicalImpact: Reversed the 2015 half-hour offset after only ~3 years. tz 2018e modeled the change at 00:00; the corrected exact instant (23:30 the prior day) required a follow-up fix in tz 2018f, so systems on 2018e had a 30-minute window of incorrect time on the transition night.

validation.supportingQuote: North Korea switches back from +0830 to +09 on 2018-05-05. ... The 2018-05-05 North Korea 30-minute time zone change took place at 23:30 the previous day, not at 00:00 that day.
validation.corrections: technicalImpact: the corrected exact instant (23:30 the prior day) was fixed in tz 2018f (2018-10-18), not 2018h. sourceUrl should be https://data.iana.org/time-zones/tzdb-2018f/NEWS

headline: North Korea matched South Korea's clock
blurb: North Korea moved its clocks forward 30 minutes to line up with South Korea.
story: Before a 2018 summit, North Korea moved its clocks forward by 30 minutes so it would match South Korea again. This undid a change it had made about three years earlier. The exact moment of the switch was recorded wrong at first, so a follow-up correction was needed.

title: EU proposes abolishing biannual DST clock changes (never enacted)
body: European Commission and European Parliament
whatChanged: After a public consultation drawing ~4.6 million responses, the European Commission proposed a directive on 12 Sep 2018 to end seasonal clock changes EU-wide, letting each member state choose permanent summer or winter time. The European Parliament adopted its first-reading position 26 Mar 2019 targeting 2021. The file stalled in the Council and has seen no progress since January 2020; never became law.
technicalImpact: A live, unresolved overhang for every timezone-aware system in the EU. Had it passed, dozens of Europe/* zones would have stopped DST on divergent dates per country, requiring large coordinated tz updates and breaking long-range scheduling. Because it remains pending, tz maintainers must keep the existing last-Sunday-March/October rules indefinitely.

validation.supportingQuote: On 12 September 2018, the European Commission published a proposal for a directive on discontinuing seasonal changes of time. ... While in January 2020, the Commission included the proposal in Annex III of the 2020 Commission Work Programme relating to 'Priority pending proposals', there have been no further progress in the Council on the file.

headline: Europe proposed ending clock changes
blurb: The EU proposed ending twice-a-year clock changes, but it never became law.
story: In 2018, after millions of people responded to a survey, the European Commission proposed ending seasonal clock changes across the EU. Each country would pick whether to stay on summer or winter time. The plan stalled and was never passed, so the old clock-change rules still stand.

title: Morocco adopts permanent UTC+1 with a recurring Ramadan exception (announced 2 days early)
body: Government of Morocco (Decree No. 2.18.855); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: By Decree 2.18.855 issued 26 Oct 2018, two days before the scheduled 28 Oct fall-back, Morocco abolished biannual clock changes and made UTC+1 permanent, EXCEPT that it drops to UTC+0 for the month of Ramadan and returns afterward. Because Ramadan moves ~11 days earlier each Gregorian year, the two transition dates shift annually.
technicalImpact: Africa/Casablanca now has two DST-like transitions every year on dates pinned to the Islamic lunar calendar, requiring a fresh tz release nearly every year with hand-computed Ramadan dates. The 2-day notice in 2018 plus astronomical uncertainty repeatedly forces last-minute patches; stale rules show Moroccan time an hour off during/after Ramadan.

validation.supportingQuote: Morocco switches from +00/+01 to permanent +01 effective 2018-10-28, so its clocks will not fall back as previously scheduled. (Thanks to Mohamed Essedik Najd and Brian Inglis.) [release 2018g, 2018-10-26]; and "Guess that Morocco will continue to fall back just before and spring forward just after Ramadan, the practice since 2012." [release 2018h]

headline: Morocco keeps one clock except in Ramadan
blurb: Morocco stopped seasonal clock changes but still shifts its clock during Ramadan.
story: In 2018 Morocco announced, just two days before a planned change, that it would stop switching its clocks twice a year. It kept one steady time except during the month of Ramadan, when the clock drops back by an hour and then returns. Because Ramadan falls about eleven days earlier each year, those two switch dates move every year.

title: RFC 8536: The Time Zone Information Format (TZif) standardized
body: IETF; authors Arthur David Olson, Paul Eggert (UCLA), Kenneth Murchison (FastMail)
whatChanged: Published February 2019, RFC 8536 gave the long-undocumented binary zoneinfo/TZif file format an official specification, defining versions 1, 2 and 3: transition times, UTC offsets, DST flags, zone abbreviations and optional leap-second records. Version 2+ uses 64-bit transition timestamps (vs 32-bit, 1901-2038-limited v1) plus a trailing POSIX TZ string.
technicalImpact: Provided an interoperability contract for the compiled files produced by zic and consumed by libc, the JVM and others. The 64-bit v2/v3 format is the key mitigation for the 2038 problem in time-zone resolution, allowing correct local-time computation far beyond 2038.

validation.supportingQuote: RFC 8536, "The Time Zone Information Format (TZif)", February 2019; authors A. Olson, P. Eggert (UCLA), and K. Murchison (FastMail). Defines TZif versions 1 (32-bit, 1901-2038), 2 (64-bit transition times + POSIX TZ footer), and 3.

headline: Time zone files got a written standard
blurb: The format of the world's time zone files finally got an official specification.
story: Computers store time zone rules in a special file format that had long gone undocumented. In 2019 an official specification was published to describe exactly how those files work. The newer version uses bigger numbers for dates, which lets software handle times correctly far into the future.

title: Brazil abolishes daylight saving time (horario de verao) indefinitely
body: Federative Republic of Brazil (Decree No. 9.772, signed by President Bolsonaro), encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Decree 9.772 of 2019-04-25 ended Brazil's southern-region DST, which had run since 1985. No spring-forward in November 2019 and none since; affected zones (America/Sao_Paulo) stay on standard time year-round (Sao Paulo permanent UTC-3).
technicalImpact: DST rules for Brazil's southern zones had to be removed. Un-updated systems performed the cancelled 2019-11 spring-forward, putting Brazil an hour ahead, hitting Carnival/holiday scheduling and banking. Because Brazil's DST start had historically floated by yearly decree, libraries had long guessed future dates, making stale guesses likely to fire after abolition until tzdata 2019b propagated.

validation.supportingQuote: Daylight Saving Time has been permanently abolished in Brazil, according to the Senate bulletin of April 25, 2019. ... São Paulo observes Brasília Time all year with no Daylight Saving Time clock changes.

headline: Brazil ended daylight saving time
blurb: Brazil stopped changing its clocks for summer, ending a practice from 1985.
story: In 2019 Brazil ended daylight saving time, which parts of the country had used since 1985. There was no clock change that November, and the affected areas have stayed on one time ever since. Systems that were not updated kept making the old change and showed Brazil an hour ahead.

title: IANA tz database adds an Expires line to the leapseconds file (release 2020a)
body: IANA / tz database maintainers
whatChanged: tzdb 2020a: zic -L gained support for an Expires line in the leapseconds file and truncates TZif output accordingly, so a TZif file built with 'right' (leap-aware) zones records when its leap-second data becomes stale instead of being silently trusted forever.
technicalImpact: For systems using 'right/' zoneinfo or feeding the leapseconds file to NTP, an expired file now signals newer leap-second info may be missing rather than continuing with possibly-wrong UTC-TAI offsets. An expired leapseconds file is a known operational footgun.

validation.supportingQuote: zic -L now supports an Expires line in the leapseconds file, and truncates the TZif output accordingly.

headline: Leap second files got an expiry date
blurb: The time database added an expiry marker to its leap second data.
story: Some systems rely on a file that lists added seconds in the world clock. In 2020 the time database added a way to mark when that file's data goes out of date. Now an expired file warns that newer information may be missing, instead of being trusted forever.

title: Negative leap second risk as Earth's rotation accelerates
body: IERS / scientific community; risk popularized by Meta, Google, NIST and geophysicists
whatChanged: Since 2020 Earth has repeatedly set records for shortest day (2020-07-19 was 1.47 ms short; further records in 2022, 2024, 2025). For the first time, length-of-day trends point toward UTC needing a NEGATIVE leap second, removing a second so the minute jumps from 23:59:58 to 00:00:00, skipping 23:59:59, rather than the historically-only-positive insertion.
technicalImpact: A negative leap second has never occurred and essentially no production NTP servers, OS kernels, time libraries, or smear schemes have been exercised against it. Smear implementations, ntpd/chrony state machines, and code assuming time never moves backward (the Cloudflare 2017 bug class) are all untested for the negative case, raising Y2K-style failure concerns.

validation.supportingQuote: Earth is spinning so fast that global timekeepers are considering something that's never been done before: adding a negative leap second. ... This follows a trend that has been observed since 2020.

headline: Earth is spinning slightly faster
blurb: The Earth has been spinning a little faster, which may force a never-tried clock fix.
story: Since 2020 the Earth has set records for its shortest days. For the first time, this points toward removing a second from the world clock rather than adding one. A removal like this has never happened, so the software and systems that keep time have never been tested against it.

title: 2021 zone-merging controversy and fork threat; tzdb 2021b release
body: TZ Coordinator Paul Eggert vs. tz community (notably Stephen Colebourne, Joda-Time/JSR-310 author); IANA tz project
whatChanged: Eggert proposed merging zones whose timestamps agree since 1970 into aliases of one canonical zone (e.g. Europe/Oslo, Stockholm, Amsterdam becoming links to Europe/Berlin), dropping distinct pre-1970 history. The plan sparked threats to fork tzdb. The 2021b release (2021-09-25), driven by urgent DST changes in Samoa and Jordan, applied only some merges and omitted most controversial 1970-agreement merges after pushback.
technicalImpact: Aliasing previously independent zone IDs breaks software storing or enumerating those IDs as canonical: Colebourne warned Joda-Time users could no longer hold a zone ID like Europe/Oslo as a first-class object, and ICU/CLDR display-name and round-tripping behavior changes. Forced downstream libraries and packagers to decide whether to follow upstream merges or carry patches.

validation.supportingQuote: "Merge more location-based Zones whose timestamps agree since 1970, as pre-1970 timestamps are out of scope." ... "Jordan now starts DST on February's last Thursday. Samoa no longer observes DST."

headline: A plan to merge time zones caused a fight
blurb: A proposal to combine similar time zones sparked a dispute among maintainers.
story: In 2021 the time database's editor proposed merging zones that have kept the same time since 1970, treating several as one. The plan dropped some older history and upset many users, with some threatening to split off their own version. A release that year applied only part of the plan after the pushback.

title: Microsoft Exchange FIP-FS 'Y2K22' int32 date overflow
body: Microsoft (Exchange Server FIP-FS / scanning engine)
whatChanged: The FIP-FS anti-malware scanning engine version/signature date was stored as a yyMMddHHmm value in a signed 32-bit integer. At 2022-01-01 the value became 2201010001, exceeding the signed int32 max of 2,147,483,647, so the conversion to a long failed.
technicalImpact: On-premises Exchange 2016/2019 mailbox-role servers stopped delivering mail at local midnight on 2022-01-01; messages queued and Event Log error 1106 reported "Can't convert '2201010001' to long". Microsoft shipped an emergency reset script and a permanent build using a larger version number.

validation.supportingQuote: Error Code: 0x80004005. Error Description: Can't convert "2201010001" to long. ... dates in 2022 have a minimum value of 2,201,010,001, which is greater than the maximum value that can be stored in the signed int32 variable.

headline: A date number broke Microsoft email
blurb: On New Year's Day 2022 a date stored as a number grew too big for Microsoft Exchange.
story: Microsoft Exchange stored a date as a single number for its malware scanner. When 2022 began, that number grew larger than the software could hold, so the check failed. On-site Exchange servers stopped delivering mail until Microsoft released a fix.

title: Sunshine Protection Act passes US Senate (permanent DST proposal)
body: US Senate (117th Congress), S.623, sponsored by Sen. Marco Rubio
whatChanged: The Senate passed S.623 by unanimous consent on 2022-03-15, which would make daylight saving time the permanent, year-round standard time across the US (states already exempt would stay on standard time), eliminating the biannual clock change. The bill stalled in the House and was never enacted; reintroduced in subsequent Congresses.
technicalImpact: Not encoded in any tz release because it never became law, the engineering lesson is the danger of premature timezone changes: had it passed near its effective date it would have required removing all US DST transitions, echoing the short-lead-time scramble of 2007. A standing reminder to track legislative status, not assume DST rules are stable.

validation.supportingQuote: "The Sunshine Protection Act S.623 passed the Senate with an amendment by Unanimous Consent on March 15, 2022 ... is 'An Act to make daylight saving time permanent' and was sponsored by Marco Rubio in the 117th Congress ... while the bill passed the Senate on March 15, 2022, it was never passed by the House." (per congress.gov actions / GovTrack)

headline: US Senate voted for permanent daylight time
blurb: The US Senate passed a bill to make daylight saving time year-round, but it stalled.
story: In 2022 the US Senate passed a bill to make daylight saving time permanent across the country and end the twice-a-year clock change. The bill stalled in the House and never became law. It has been brought back in later sessions of Congress.

title: Meta calls to retire the leap second (industry coalition with Google, Microsoft, Amazon, NIST, BIPM)
body: Meta (Facebook) Engineering, with stated support from Google, Microsoft, Amazon, US NIST and BIPM
whatChanged: Meta published 'It's time to leave the leap second in the past', publicly advocating to stop introducing new leap seconds and standardize on continuous time, coalescing major cloud/tech vendors behind UTC reform months before the CGPM 2022 vote.
technicalImpact: Documents the operational case: leap seconds are rare, poorly tested events that 'devastate the community every time'. Promotes smearing as the de-facto mitigation and frames the negative leap second as an unacceptable, untested risk, directly motivating vendor smear standards and the push for abolition.

validation.supportingQuote: we're supporting an industry effort to stop future introductions of leap seconds and stay at a current level of 27

headline: Tech giants called to drop leap seconds
blurb: Meta and other major companies urged the world to stop adding leap seconds.
story: In 2022 Meta published a call to stop adding leap seconds to the world clock, backed by other large technology companies and standards bodies. They argued that these rare changes are hard to test and cause repeated trouble. They pointed to smoothing the change over time as a better approach and warned against ever removing a second.

title: Iran permanently abolishes daylight saving time
body: Islamic Consultative Assembly (Majlis) and Guardian Council of Iran; encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: Iran's parliament approved a bill abolishing DST on 15 Mar 2022; promulgated by President Raisi on 22 May 2022. Iran observed its final fall-back on 21 Sep 2022 and stays on permanent standard time UTC+3:30 (IRST) year-round, ending decades of half-hour seasonal shifts to/from UTC+4:30.
technicalImpact: tz 2022b removed all future DST transitions for Asia/Tehran. Unpatched systems wrongly apply DST each March/September, putting Iranian time 60 minutes off for months. Combined with Iran's unusual +3:30 base offset, a frequent source of scheduling errors.

validation.supportingQuote: However, on 21 September 2022, Iran abolished daylight saving time effective immediately and now observes standard time year-round. The plan was approved by the Parliament on 15 March 2022.
validation.corrections: tzRelease should be "2022b" (released 2022-08-10), not "2022e". Correspondingly, technicalImpact should read "tz 2022b removed all future DST transitions for Asia/Tehran" instead of "tz 2022e".

headline: Iran ended daylight saving time
blurb: Iran stopped its twice-a-year clock changes and settled on one time.
story: Iran's parliament approved ending daylight saving time in 2022, and the country made its final clock change that September. Iran now stays on one time all year, ending decades of seasonal half-hour shifts. Systems that were not updated keep making the old changes and show Iranian time an hour off for months at a time.

title: Mexico abolishes nationwide daylight saving time
body: Mexican Congress (approved 2022-10-26/27); signed into the Ley de los Husos Horarios by President Lopez Obrador
whatChanged: Most of Mexico ended DST, with the last clock change on 2022-10-30. Exceptions kept on a US-aligned DST schedule: all of Baja California and northern border municipalities in Chihuahua, Coahuila, Nuevo Leon, and Tamaulipas, to preserve alignment with adjacent US zones.
technicalImpact: Encoded in tz 2022f (2022-10-28), days before the effective date, a textbook short-lead-time emergency update. America/Mexico_City, /Monterrey, /Mazatlan etc. stopped observing DST; Chihuahua shifted to year-round -06. Systems not on 2022f computed Mexican time an hour wrong from 2022-10-30, and the border carve-outs broke single-zone assumptions.

validation.supportingQuote: Mexico will no longer observe DST after 2022, except for areas near the US border that continue to observe US DST rules. On 2022-10-30 at 02:00 the Mexican state of Chihuahua moves from -07 (-06 with DST) to year-round -06, thus not changing its clocks that day.
validation.corrections: Optional precision: the tz 2022f release is dated 2022-10-29 in the icann.org tz-announce header (event body says 2022-10-28); IANA NEWS uses -0700 timestamps so this is a one-day rounding nuance, not an error in the core event.

headline: Mexico stops changing its clocks
blurb: Most of Mexico made its last seasonal clock change and dropped daylight saving time.
story: In October 2022, most of Mexico ended daylight saving time after one final clock change. A few areas near the United States border kept changing their clocks to stay in step with their neighbors. The rule lists were updated just days before the change took effect, so any computer that missed the update showed the wrong time.

title: Amazon Time Sync goes public over the internet with 24-hour leap smear
body: Amazon Web Services (AWS)
whatChanged: AWS made Amazon Time Sync available as a public NTP service at time.aws.com, backed by satellite-connected atomic reference clocks, and documented that leap seconds are absorbed via a 24-hour linear smear from noon to noon UTC (matching Google's standard).
technicalImpact: Extends smeared, leap-second-free time to IoT and on-prem clients beyond AWS, further entrenching smearing as the production answer to leap seconds. Mixing AWS/Google smeared sources with non-smeared (leaping) NTP risks sub-second clock disagreement during a leap event.

validation.supportingQuote: In the event of a leap second, the Amazon Time Sync service automatically handles this for you by smoothing out the addition, or removal, of the leap second with a 24-hour linear smear from noon to noon UTC.
validation.corrections: date: 2022-11-10 (cited aws.amazon.com What's New page posted Nov 10, 2022; not 2022-11-21)

headline: Amazon opens its clock service to everyone
blurb: Amazon let anyone use its time service, which spreads out extra seconds slowly.
story: In November 2022, Amazon made its time service available to anyone on the internet, not just its own customers. The service is backed by very accurate reference clocks. When an extra second needs to be added, it spreads that second out gradually over a full day instead of adding it all at once.

title: CGPM Resolution 4 (2022): phase out leap seconds by or before 2035
body: 27th General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM), Versailles
whatChanged: CGPM adopted Resolution 4 'On the use and future development of UTC', deciding that the maximum allowed value of |UT1-UTC| will be increased in or before 2035 (from the current ~0.9s), directing CIPM (with ITU) to propose a new tolerance ensuring UTC continuity for at least a century. In effect this ends the regular insertion of leap seconds. The US and France led; Russia voted against citing GLONASS impact.
technicalImpact: After 2035 no new leap seconds will be added for decades, removing the recurring 23:59:60 discontinuity that has caused outages. TAI-UTC will eventually become a fixed constant, leap-seconds.list will stop growing, and 'right'/POSIX zoneinfo divergence freezes. The resolution cites risks to GNSS, telecom, and energy infrastructure and flags the untested negative-leap-second case. Software hardcoding the 0.9s tolerance or expecting future leap seconds must be updated.

validation.supportingQuote: "On the use and future development of UTC", "the maximum value for the difference (UT1-UTC) will be increased in, or before, 2035" with CIPM directed to consult ITU and propose a new value ensuring "continuity of UTC for at least a century."

headline: World agrees to phase out leap seconds
blurb: An international body voted to stop adding occasional extra seconds by 2035.
story: In 2022, an international group that oversees measurement agreed to let clock time drift a little further from the sun's time. This decision effectively ends the practice of adding occasional extra seconds, with the change due by 2035. The group asked others to work out the exact details. Most members agreed, though Russia voted against it.

title: Lebanon splits into two simultaneous time zones over a last-minute DST delay
body: Government of Lebanon (caretaker PM Najib Mikati); encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: On 23 Mar 2023, two days before the scheduled spring-forward, caretaker PM Mikati decreed DST be postponed from 25/26 Mar to 20/21 Apr. Christian institutions and broadcasters rejected the decree and moved clocks forward anyway, so from 26 Mar the country ran on two parallel times an hour apart ('Muslim time' UTC+2 and 'Christian time' UTC+3). The government reversed itself, making DST effective from midnight 29/30 Mar.
technicalImpact: A real-world demonstration of timezone fragmentation. IANA shipped 2023b (23 Mar) encoding the Apr delay; on 28 Mar shipped 2023c which modeled the chaos by reverting to tzdb 2023a data. Phones, banks, airlines and hospital systems disagreed on the hour; carriers told users to disable NTP and set clocks manually, breaking calendars. Downstream Linux distros lagged a week behind tz releases, making manual intervention unavoidable.

validation.supportingQuote: 2023b: "This year Lebanon springs forward April 20/21 not March 25/26. (Thanks to Saadallah Itani.) [This was reverted in 2023c.]" | 2023c (2023-03-28): "Model Lebanon's DST chaos by reverting data to tzdb 2023a. (Thanks to Rany Hany for the heads-up.)"

headline: Lebanon ran on two times at once
blurb: A last-minute clock decision split Lebanon into two times an hour apart.
story: In March 2023, just days before clocks were due to move forward, the government delayed the change by several weeks. Many institutions rejected the decision and moved their clocks forward anyway. For a few days the country ran on two times that were an hour apart. The government soon reversed itself and let the clock change happen.

title: Egypt reintroduces DST in 2023 on short notice
body: Government of Egypt; encoded by IANA tz
whatChanged: After abandoning DST entirely from 2015, Egypt reinstated it in 2023, observing DST from the last Friday of April through the last Thursday of October. Confirmed only weeks before the 28 Apr 2023 spring-forward.
technicalImpact: Required tz 2023a (2023-03-22) to encode the new rule plus a Ramadan suspension whose dates float on the lunar calendar. Like Morocco, this makes Africa/Cairo require near-annual hand-maintained transition dates; the short lead time risked unpatched systems being an hour off for the Egyptian summer.

validation.supportingQuote: The Egyptian government decided on Wednesday to restore applying daylight-saving time annually, starting from April's last Friday and until October's last Thursday, with the aim of "rationalising energy use"; the change will be lifted during Ramadan.
validation.corrections: Remove the Ramadan carve-out entirely. Corrected whatChanged: "After abandoning DST entirely from 2015, Egypt reinstated it in 2023, observing DST from the last Friday of April through the last Thursday of October. Unlike pre-2015 practice (and unlike Morocco/Palestine), the 2023 policy did NOT suspend DST during Ramadan. Confirmed only weeks before the 28 Apr 2023 spring-forward." Corrected technicalImpact: "Required tz 2023a (2023-03-22) to encode the new fixed rule (last Friday of April to last Thursday of October). No Ramadan suspension is encoded for Africa/Cairo, the rule is algorithmic and needs no annual hand-maintenance (contrast Morocco/Palestine). The short lead time risked unpatched systems being an hour off for the Egyptian summer." Title should drop "with a Ramadan carve-out."

headline: Egypt brings back seasonal clock changes
blurb: Egypt started changing its clocks again after years without it.
story: Egypt had stopped changing its clocks back in 2015. In 2023 it decided to bring seasonal clock changes back, confirming the plan only weeks before it began. The country also pauses the change during Ramadan, and those dates shift each year, which makes the rules harder to keep up to date.

title: Agnew (Nature, 2024): negative leap second projected ~2029, postponed by ice melt
body: Duncan Agnew, Scripps Institution of Oceanography (UC San Diego); published in Nature
whatChanged: Peer-reviewed analysis of Earth's core/mantle angular-velocity trends concluded UTC as currently defined would require its first-ever negative leap second by about 2029, and that polar ice melt from global warming has delayed this by roughly three years (otherwise ~2026).
technicalImpact: Gives a concrete near-term date for the untested negative-leap-second scenario, potentially BEFORE the 2035 abolition takes effect. Pressures the timekeeping community to either implement a negative leap second on systems never designed for it or accelerate the UTC-tolerance change, and strengthens the case for retiring leap seconds entirely before 2029.

validation.supportingQuote: Agnew calculated that a "negative leap second" event would be needed by 2029, the first in history; without the effects of climate change, Earth would need that negative leap second in 2026 instead of 2029 (Duncan Agnew, Nature, published 2024-03-27).

headline: Study warns clocks may lose a second
blurb: A study said clocks may need to drop a second for the first time around 2029.
story: A 2024 study looked at how fast the Earth is spinning. It concluded that official time may need to remove a second for the first time ever, around 2029. The study also found that melting polar ice has slowed the Earth a little and pushed that date back by about three years. Removing a second has never been done before, so it is an untested situation.

title: RFC 9636: Updated TZif specification, obsoletes RFC 8536
body: IETF; lead author Kenneth Murchison (FastMail) with Arthur David Olson and Paul Eggert
whatChanged: Published 2024-10-01, RFC 9636 replaced and obsoleted RFC 8536 as the authoritative TZif specification, refining and clarifying the version 1/2/3 binary format and its registered media types while remaining upward-compatible with deployed files.
technicalImpact: Supersedes RFC 8536 as the document implementations should cite when reading/writing zoneinfo files; the tz project updated its documentation to reference RFC 9636. Provides current, corrected guidance for parser authors, reducing ambiguity-driven bugs in TZif handling.

validation.supportingQuote: "This document replaces and obsoletes RFC 8536, providing editorial improvements, new details, and errata fixes while keeping full compatibility with the interchange format of RFC 8536." (header: "Request for Comments: 9636 / Obsoletes: 8536 / ... K. Murchison / Fastmail / October 2024")

headline: New standard for time zone files
blurb: A new technical document updated the rules for time zone data files.
story: In October 2024, a new technical document replaced an older one as the official guide for how time zone data files are written and read. It clarified and refined the existing format while staying compatible with files already in use. The time zone project updated its own notes to point to the new document, giving software authors clearer guidance.

title: IERS Bulletin C: no leap second 2017 onward; TAI-UTC frozen at 37s through 2025
body: IERS, Earth Orientation Centre
whatChanged: Following the last positive leap second (2016-12-31), every subsequent semi-annual Bulletin C has announced NO leap second. Bulletin C 70 (July 2025) again confirmed no leap second at end-2025, keeping UTC-TAI = -37s unchanged since 2017-01-01.
technicalImpact: A nearly decade-long pause means leap-second handling code paths have not been exercised in production since 2016, so latent bugs sit untested, and any future leap second, especially the projected negative one, would land on under-rehearsed systems. leap-seconds.list and zoneinfo leap data have not changed since 2016.

validation.supportingQuote: "NO leap second will be introduced at the end of June 2026. ... UTC-TAI = -37 s", Bulletin C, Earth Orientation Center of IERS (Observatoire de Paris), signed by Christian Bizouard.
validation.corrections: date should be 2025-07-07 (Bulletin C 70 published 7 July 2025, not 4 July 2025)

headline: No extra second added for years
blurb: No extra second has been added to official time since the end of 2016.
story: The last extra second was added at the end of 2016. Every check since then has confirmed that no extra second was needed, including the one made in July 2025. Because the gap has lasted so long, the software that handles these extra seconds has not been used in real life for years, so any hidden problems remain untested.

title: 28th CGPM (2026): scheduled decision on the new UT1-UTC tolerance
body: CGPM / CIPM, in consultation with ITU and other organizations
whatChanged: Resolution 4 (2022) directs CIPM to propose a specific new maximum UT1-UTC value and an implementation plan, and to draft a resolution for agreement at the 28th CGPM (2026). The meeting at which the concrete tolerance and the path to 2035 are expected to be formally set.
technicalImpact: Will fix the numeric tolerance determining how far civil UTC may diverge from solar time before any correction, defining whether/when systems must ever apply a UT1-UTC offset, and confirming the timeline after which leap-second code can be safely retired. Drives long-horizon planning for GNSS, astronomy, and OS/NTP maintainers.

validation.supportingQuote: The resolution requests CIPM to "propose a new maximum value for the difference (UT1-UTC) that will ensure the continuity of UTC for at least a century" and to "draft a resolution including these proposals for agreement at the 28th meeting of the CGPM (2026)"; it also "decides that the maximum value for the difference (UT1-UTC) will be increased in, or before, 2035."

headline: A 2026 meeting will set the new limit
blurb: A 2026 meeting is set to decide how far clock time may drift from the sun.
story: A 2022 decision asked experts to propose exactly how far official clock time may drift from the sun's time, along with a plan to carry it out. A meeting in 2026 is the place where that exact limit and the path toward 2035 are expected to be settled. The outcome will guide long-term planning for navigation, astronomy, and computer systems.

title: IANA tz code adds -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 and a never-expiring leapseconds file
body: IANA / tz database maintainers
whatChanged: Recent tzdb releases added the compile option -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 to disable runtime leap-second support, and made leapseconds.awk able to emit a leapseconds file that never expires. NEWS frames these as preparation for leap seconds being discontinued.
technicalImpact: Gives OS and library builders a supported path to compile out leap-second handling and ship a non-expiring leapseconds file once leap seconds stop, reducing the surface area of leap-second-specific bugs.

validation.supportingQuote: Release 2026a - 2026-03-01: "Compiling with the new option -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 disables runtime support for leap seconds. Although this conforms to POSIX, shrinks tzcode's attack surface, and is more efficient, it fails to support Internet RFC 9636's leap seconds." / Release 2021b - 2021-09-24: "leapseconds.awk can now process a leap seconds file that never expires; this might be useful if leap seconds are discontinued."
validation.corrections: date: 2026-03-01 (release 2026a), not 2025-12-10. tzRelease: 2026a, not 2025c. The -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 option was added in tzdb 2026a (2026-03-01). The leapseconds.awk never-expiring-file capability predates this, added in 2021b (2021-09-24). body/responsible body (IANA/tz maintainers) and technical impact are correct.

headline: Time data adds a switch to skip extra seconds
blurb: Time zone software gained an option to leave out support for extra seconds.
story: Recent updates to the widely used time zone software added an option to turn off support for occasional extra seconds. They also made it possible to produce a file of past extra seconds that never expires. The notes describe these changes as preparation for a future where extra seconds are no longer added.

title: IANA tzdb makes leap-second ('right') support optional and non-default
body: IANA Time Zone Database (tzdb) maintainers
whatChanged: Reflecting the planned 2035 discontinuation, tzdb's 2026a release stopped installing the leap-second-aware 'right' TZif files by default and added a compile option (-DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0) to disable runtime leap-second support entirely. leapseconds/leap-seconds.list is still distributed.
technicalImpact: Downstream OS/distro packagers building from tzdb get POSIX (leap-ignoring) behavior by default and must opt in for TAI-counting 'right' zones. Cements the ecosystem assumption that no new leap seconds are coming and simplifies the path to fixed TAI-UTC, while preserving historical leap data for systems needing true TAI timestamps.

validation.supportingQuote: The 'right' TZif files are no longer installed by default. ... Compiling with the new option -DTZ_RUNTIME_LEAPS=0 disables runtime support for leap seconds. Although this conforms to POSIX, shrinks tzcode's attack surface, and is more efficient, it fails to support Internet RFC 9636's leap seconds.
validation.corrections: Date should be 2026-03-01 (release timestamp 2026-03-02 UTC), not 2026-04-22. The 2026-04-22 2026a re-roll introduced no leap-second/'right' changes; it covered British Columbia's permanent -07 switch and zic overflow fixes. Releaser: Paul Eggert (IANA tzdb).

headline: Time data drops extra seconds by default
blurb: Time zone software stopped including extra-second support unless you ask for it.
story: A 2026 update to the widely used time zone software stopped including its extra-second mode by default. It also added an option to turn that support off entirely. The older data is still available for anyone who needs it. This reflects the expectation that no new extra seconds are coming.

title: British Columbia adopts permanent standard offset (UTC-07 year-round)
body: Government of British Columbia, Canada (encoded in IANA tz 2026b)
whatChanged: British Columbia abolished seasonal clock changes. After its final spring-forward in 2026, the province stays on UTC-07 permanently; the change legally took effect 2026-03-09, and tzdata temporarily models the permanent offset at a later transition.
technicalImpact: America/Vancouver and BC zones lose the autumn 2026 fall-back. Systems without tzdata 2026b read one hour behind through the winter, diverging from updated systems.

validation.supportingQuote: British Columbia moved to permanent -07 on 2026-03-09.

headline: British Columbia stops changing its clocks
blurb: British Columbia ended seasonal clock changes and kept one time all year.
story: British Columbia decided to stop changing its clocks twice a year. After one final spring clock change in 2026, the province stays on the same time all year. Computers that did not get the updated time data would read an hour behind through the winter, putting them out of step with updated systems.

title: NTP Era 0 rollover (unsigned 32-bit seconds since 1900)
body: NTP Project / IETF (RFC 5905); originally David L. Mills
whatChanged: NTP timestamps are 64-bit fixed-point: a 32-bit seconds field counting from the NTP epoch (1900-01-01T00:00:00Z) plus a 32-bit fraction. The unsigned 32-bit seconds field overflows after 2^32 seconds (~136 years), rolling from Era 0 to Era 1 at 2036-02-07T06:28:16Z.
technicalImpact: Implementations ignoring the era number wrap to 1900 (and the wrap precedes the 2038 Unix limit, so it hits first). Clients booting with a wildly wrong clock can mis-infer the era, refuse to sync, or step by ~136 years. RFC 5905's era/era-offset arithmetic mitigates it when implemented correctly.

validation.supportingQuote: "This format can represent the 136 years from 1900 to 2036 with a precision of 232 ps." / "The concern now is a possible meltdown when the ... NTP timestamp rolls over in 2036." / Table 1 shows the transition from era 0 to era 1 between 7 Feb 2036 and 8 Feb 2036.

headline: A clock counter runs out in 2036
blurb: A common network clock format reaches the end of its count in 2036.
story: Many computers keep time using a counter that started in 1900. That counter has a limit it reaches in early 2036, after which it starts over. Software that does not track which cycle it is in can jump back to 1900, refuse to sync, or set the clock off by well over a century. Correctly written software handles the reset.

title: Unix Year 2038 problem (signed 32-bit time_t overflow)
body: Unix/POSIX ecosystem; ISO C / The Open Group (POSIX time_t); Linux kernel and glibc maintainers driving 64-bit time_t
whatChanged: POSIX time counts seconds since the Unix epoch (1970-01-01T00:00:00Z) in a signed 32-bit integer. The max value 2,147,483,647 s is reached at 2038-01-19T03:14:07Z; the next second overflows to -2^31, interpreted as 1901-12-13T20:45:52Z.
technicalImpact: Any 32-bit time_t (or 32-bit storage of Unix timestamps) wraps to 1901, breaking timers, certificate validity, filesystem timestamps, schedulers, and embedded controllers. Remediation is widening time_t to signed 64-bit; Linux kernel and glibc 2.32+ provide 64-bit time on 32-bit platforms, but embedded/legacy 32-bit devices remain at risk.

validation.supportingQuote: "03:14:07 UTC on 19 January 2038 ... Unix time is defined as the number of seconds elapsed since 00:00:00 UTC on 1 January 1970 ... stored as a signed 32-bit integer ... Systems will interpret as 20:45:52 UTC on 13 December 1901 ... Modern systems and software updates address this problem by using signed 64-bit integers."
validation.corrections: Optional precision fix: 64-bit time_t opt-in on 32-bit platforms (_TIME_BITS=64) became fully usable in glibc 2.34 (2021) with Linux kernel 5.1+, not 2.32. Underlying __time64_t plumbing landed in glibc 2.32.

headline: A clock counter runs out in 2038
blurb: A common computer time counter reaches its limit in early 2038.
story: Many computers count time as seconds since 1970, stored in a way that runs out in early 2038. When it overflows, the time wraps back to 1901. This can break timers, security certificates, file dates, and schedules. The fix is to store the count in a larger format, which newer systems already do, but many older and embedded devices remain at risk.
